name: 7-Pot Barrackapore
description: The *7-Pot Barrackpore* is a super-hot Trinidadian pepper reaching around **1,000,000–1,300,000 SHU**, known for its blistering heat and rugged, wrinkled pods. Its flavour is fruity with a slightly earthy bitterness, typical of many *Capsicum chinense* varieties. You’ll often find it in shades of red or chocolate, with elongated pods that sometimes develop a small “stinger.” More details about similar 7-Pot heat profiles can be found at [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-pepper/). For general super-hot characteristics, see [ChiliPepperMadness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/).
aditionalInfo: The name “**7-Pot**” comes from the folklore that a single pod can season **seven pots of stew**, a story widely repeated in Caribbean pepper culture and referenced on [PepperGeek](https://peppergeek.com/hot-pepper-scoville-scale/). The “**Barrackpore**” part refers to the town of **Barrackpore, Trinidad**, where this variant was identified; the town itself was named after Barrackpore in India, creating a unique cross-continental naming lineage documented on [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Barrackpore,_Trinidad_and_Tobago).
shortDescription: Super-hot Trinidad pepper with fruity and slightly earthy flavour and fierce heat.

name: Pepper X
description: **Pepper X** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super-hot chili pepper** developed by **Ed Currie** of the *PuckerButt Pepper Company* in South Carolina, USA. It averages around **2.69 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it the **world’s hottest certified pepper**. The fruit is **wrinkled, elongated, and green-yellow to mustard-yellow**, used mainly for **extreme hot sauces** rather than casual consumption ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pepper_X?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Pepper X”** began as a **placeholder** during development, used while legal and trademark issues were pending. Currie chose the **“X”** to symbolize both the **unknown** and **extreme** qualities of this pepper ([Reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/spicy/comments/179hz0i?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). It was created by **crossbreeding the Carolina Reaper** with another ultra-hot, undisclosed variety over nearly a decade, aiming to break heat records. The name conveys **mystery, danger, and innovation** in modern chili breeding ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pepper_X?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
shortDescription: Pepper X is an extremely hot chili from the US with legendary heat, used mostly in sauces.

name: Carolina Reaper
description: **Carolina Reaper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **extreme super-hot chili pepper** developed by **Ed Currie** of the *PuckerButt Pepper Company* in South Carolina. It averages **1.64 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, with peaks exceeding **2.2 million SHU**, making it one of the **world’s hottest peppers**. The fruit is **small, red, and bumpy**, often with a scorpion-like tail, and is prized for its **fruity flavor beneath its searing heat** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carolina_Reaper?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/carolina-reaper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Carolina Reaper”** reflects both its **origin** and **lethal heat level**. “Carolina” honors its birthplace in **South Carolina**, while “Reaper” symbolizes its **intense spiciness**, suggesting it could “reap” the unprepared consumer ([Guinness World Records](https://www.guinnessworldrecords.com/world-records/hottest-chili?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Currie bred it by crossing a **Pakistani Naga pepper** with a **Red Habanero**, spending years refining its flavor and potency. The result became **a global icon of extreme heat and chili culture** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carolina_Reaper?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
shortDescription: The Carolina Reaper is the world's hottest pepper, bred by Ed Curlin. It has a fruity, sweet flavour before an intense, long-lasting burn.

name: Trinidad Moruga Scorpion
description: **Trinidad Moruga Scorpion** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **ultra-hot chili pepper** originating from the **Moruga District in Trinidad and Tobago**. It averages around **1.2–2 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)** and has a **fruity, slightly smoky flavor** beneath its intense burn. The pepper’s **wrinkled red pods** often feature a **stinger-like tail**, inspiring its “scorpion” nickname. It’s a favorite among chili enthusiasts seeking extreme heat for sauces and competitions ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-moruga-scorpion/?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinidad_Moruga_scorpion?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Trinidad Moruga Scorpion”** combines its **place of origin—Moruga, Trinidad—with its stinger-like shape and deadly heat** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/trinidad-moruga-scorpion/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The term “Scorpion” refers to the pepper’s **tail-like tip** resembling a scorpion’s barb. Developed and identified by the **University of Trinidad and Tobago**, it once held the title of the **world’s hottest pepper** (2012). The name symbolizes **Trinidadian pride** and the pepper’s **formidable power** in the chili world ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinidad_Moruga_scorpion?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
shortDescription: Ultra-hot Trinidad pepper with fruity, smoky notes and powerful, lingering burn.

name: Trinidad Scorpion Chocolate
description: **Trinidad Scorpion Chocolate** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **ultra-hot chili pepper** known for its **dark brown color** and **intense heat**, averaging **1.2 to 2 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Native to **Trinidad and Tobago**, it has a **smoky, earthy flavor** with subtle sweetness beneath its fiery punch. The pods are **wrinkled and stinger-tipped**, resembling a scorpion’s tail, and are commonly used in **hot sauces, powders, and marinades** ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-chocolate/?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/trinidad-scorpion/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Trinidad Scorpion Chocolate”** combines its **geographic origin**, **distinctive stinger-like shape**, and **deep chocolate-brown hue**. “Scorpion” references the **sharp, curved tip** of the pepper, while “Chocolate” describes its **rich, dark coloration** when mature ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-chocolate/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Originating from **Trinidad’s Moruga region**, it is a natural color variant of the red Trinidad Scorpion. The name conveys both **danger and allure**, capturing its **intense heat and exotic appearance** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trinidad_Moruga_scorpion?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
shortDescription: Dark, smoky Trinidad scorpion pepper with intense heat and earthy undertones.

name: 7 Pot Brain Strain
description: **7 Pot Brain Strain** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super-hot pepper** originating from **Trinidad**, famous for its **gnarled, brain-like surface** and extreme heat—often exceeding **1.2 to 1.4 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It has a **fruity, slightly floral flavor** that adds both **sweetness and fiery intensity** to sauces and salsas. The pods are typically **bright red, thick-walled, and irregularly shaped**, with a heat level that rivals the world’s hottest peppers ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-brain-strain/?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/7-pot-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Brain Strain”** reflects both **its appearance and power**. “7 Pot” refers to its Trinidadian origin—locals claim one pepper can **spice seven pots of stew**. The “Brain Strain” part was coined by chili grower **David Capiello**, who selectively bred the most wrinkled pods to resemble **the folds of a human brain** ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-brain-strain/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). The result is one of the **most intimidating and visually striking super-hots** in existence ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/7-pot-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
shortDescription: Super-hot pepper from Trinidad with a brain-like surface and fruity punch.

name: Bedfordshire Super Naga
description: **Bedfordshire Super Naga** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **British-bred super-hot chili** reaching **900,000–1,120,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces **dark red, wrinkled pods** with a fruity flavor that builds into intense heat, making it one of the **hottest market-available peppers in the UK** ([Cayenne Diane](https://www.cayennediane.com/peppers/bedfordshire-super-naga/)). The plant is **robust and productive**, suitable for both commercial and home cultivation.
aditionalInfo: The name **“Bedfordshire Super Naga”** reflects its **origin and pedigree**. “Bedfordshire” denotes the **UK county** where grower Salvatore Genovese developed it, while “Super Naga” references the **potent Naga chili lineage from India** ([Groenten Nieuws](https://www.groentennieuws.nl/article/87644/tesco-verkoopt-heetste-peper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). “Super” emphasizes its **extreme heat** compared to standard Naga varieties. The name honors both its **geographic birthplace** and **high-spice heritage**, highlighting its **UK-grown super-hot status**.
shortDescription: British super-hot chili with fruity flavours and fierce heat.

name: Dragon's Breath
description: **Dragon’s Breath pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot cultivar claimed to reach around **2.48 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features small, deeply wrinkled, bright red pods with a fruity note beneath the searing heat. Originally developed in the UK, it has become a prized and cautionary specimen among chili‑enthusiasts, primarily used for novelty or extract rather than everyday cooking. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/dragons-breath/) , [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/dragons-breath-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Dragon’s Breath”** refers both to its **mythical magnitude of heat** and its geographic connection to Wales — home of the legendary **Welsh Dragon**. The pepper was developed in a collaboration between grower Mike Smith, farmer Neal Price and Nottingham Trent University, and the name honours its origins in St Asaph, Denbighshire, Wales. While the cultivar was initially intended for medicinal capsaicin research, its extraordinary heat earned it the fiery moniker that echoes both national mythology and spicy intensity. ([Live Science/Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dragon%27s_Breath_%28chili_pepper%29))
shortDescription: Extreme heat pepper with tiny pods and a fiery punch, mainly for extracts.

name: Dorset Naga
description: **Dorset Naga** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a super‑hot chili pepper that can reach upwards of **544,000 to over 1,221,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features wrinkled, wedge‑shaped pods that ripen from green to fiery red, and offers a **fruity aroma with intense, slow‑building heat**. Originally developed in England’s cooler climate, it remains one of the world’s most extreme peppers. ([Cayenne Diane](https://pepperscale.com/dorset-naga/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Dorset Naga” reflects both its **region of development** and its **chili lineage**. “Dorset” honours the county in England where growers Joy Michaud and Michael Michaud of Sea Spring Seeds selected the pepper from seeds of the Bangladeshi variety Naga Morich. “Naga” traces back to the original Naga chili from northeast India and Bangladesh, tying the name to its fiery heritage. Over years of selective breeding they stabilized the variety, leading to its recognition as a distinct cultivar. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/dorset-naga-chili-pepper/) , [Authority Health Mag](https://www.authorityhealthmag.com/dorset-naga-chili/) )
shortDescription: Fruity and intense British-bred super-hot chili with slow-building heat.

name: Naga Viper
description: **Naga Viper** (*Capsicum chinense × Capsicum frutescens × Capsicum chinense “Bhut Jolokia”*) is a **hybrid super-hot chili** developed in the United Kingdom. It averages around **1.3 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, with pods that are wrinkled, conical, and red at maturity. The pepper combines **fruity, smoky flavors** with extreme heat, used primarily in **hot sauces, extracts, and chili challenges** rather than culinary applications ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/naga-viper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/naga-viper-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Naga Viper”** reflects its **genetic heritage and formidable heat**. “Naga” comes from the **Bhut Jolokia (Naga) chili** of northeast India, while “Viper” emphasizes its **deadly potency and hybrid lineage**. Developed by chili breeder **Gerald Fowler in the UK**, it is a **triple hybrid** designed to combine the heat of the Naga, Trinidad Scorpion, and Bhut Jolokia. The name conveys **danger, speed, and extreme intensity**, making it instantly recognizable among super-hot chili enthusiasts ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/naga-viper-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Hybrid super-hot chili with fruity, smoky flavour and high SHU range.

name: Spanish Naga
description: **Spanish Naga Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super-hot chili cultivar** rated around **1,086,844 SHU**, with compact, round-to-tapered pods that ripen from green to red and a wrinkled, knobby surface. It has a **fruity, citrus-like aroma beneath extreme heat**, mainly used in **sauces and specialty chili applications** ([Cayenne Diane](https://www.cayennediane.com/peppers/gibralta-spanish-naga-pepper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/gibralta-spanish-naga-chili-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Spanish Naga”** (also called “Gibraltar Naga”) reflects both **geography and lineage**. Although grown in **Spain**, the pepper was developed in the **UK from Indian Naga-type peppers**. “Naga” connects it to the **fiery Indian/Bangladeshi chili family**, while “Spanish” acknowledges the **Spanish cultivation environment** that intensifies heat. The name honors **its hybrid origin, extreme potency, and geographic influences** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/gibralta-spanish-naga-chili-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
shortDescription: Super-hot Spanish Naga with a citrus-fruity aroma and serious heat.

name: Bhut Jolokia Chocolate
description: **Bhut Jolokia Chocolate** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili pepper variant boasting **800,000 to 1,001,304 Scoville Heat Units**, wrinkled pods that ripen to a deep chocolate‑brown hue, and a subtle, fruity‑smoky flavour beneath its intense heat. It’s typically used in extreme hot sauces, powders and chilli‑enthusiast cultivations. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bhut-jolokia-chocolate/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bhut Jolokia Chocolate” merges heritage and hue: “Bhut Jolokia” (Assamese: ভূত জলকীয়া) means *“ghost pepper”*, a nod to its haunting heat, while “Chocolate” describes the **natural dark‑brown coloration** of this rare variant. Originating in **northeast India** (Assam, Nagaland, Manipur), it’s considered a natural mutation of the original Red Bhut Jolokia. The name thus honours both its **fiery lineage** and its **distinctive colour variant**, making it instantly recognisable among super‑hot chilli aficionados. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Chocolate_Ghost_Chile_Pepper_14968.php))
shortDescription: Chocolate variant of the Bhut Jolokia with smoky, fruity notes and intense heat.

name: Komodo Dragon
description: **Komodo Dragon pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili pepper developed in the UK, with a heat range of approximately **1,400,000–2,200,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features small, wrinkled pods that mature from green to red and deliver a fruity flavour followed by a delayed, intense burn. It was introduced commercially in 2015 and marketed as one of the world’s hottest peppers. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/komodo-dragon-chili-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Komodo Dragon”** draws on both its intense heat and symbolic imagery. It was bred by UK grower Salvatore Genovese in Bedfordshire and named after the fierce Indonesian lizard, suggesting the pepper’s dominating power. The dramatic moniker reflects its status as the hottest commercially grown pepper in the UK at its debut, and conjures the idea of fiery, dragon‑like breath. Its name emphasises both **extreme potency** and **mythical intimidation**.

name: 7 Pot Douglah
description: **7 Pot Douglah** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a rare and extremely hot chocolate‑brown chili pepper from Trinidad, ranking between **923,889 and 1,853,986 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Its pods are wrinkled, about 2 inches long, and mature from green to dark chocolate brown. The flavor is fruity with nutty and earthy undertones, but it’s the intense, lingering heat that defines this cultivar—used primarily in super‑hot sauces and chili challenges ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/7-pot-douglah-chili-peppers/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-douglah/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Douglah”** combines the Trinidadian “7 Pot” prefix—meaning one pepper is enough to season **seven cooking pots**—with “Douglah,” a local term meaning **“mixed race person”**, referencing the pepper’s brown coloration. “Douglah” in Trinidadian slang reflects its distinctive chocolate brown hue, setting it apart from the red 7 Pot varieties. The naming thus honours both its legendary heat‑capacity and its cultural roots in Trinidadian chili‑growing tradition ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-douglah/)).
shortDescription: Rare chocolate-brown Trinidad pepper with fruity, nutty, and earthy flavors and intense heat.

name: Naga Morich
description: **Naga Morich** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an **extremely hot chili pepper** from **northeastern India and Bangladesh**, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **800,000 to over 1,000,000 SHU**. It produces small, wrinkled, bright red pods with a **fruity, slightly smoky flavor**. Primarily used in hot sauces, powders, and spicy dishes, it is recognized as one of the hottest peppers in the world ([SuperHotChiles](https://www.superhotchiles.com/product/naga-morich-pepper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/naga-morich/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Naga Morich”** combines the **ethnic Naga people of India’s northeast** and the word *“morich”*, meaning **chili pepper** in Bengali. It highlights the pepper’s **geographic and cultural roots**. Known locally for its **exceptional heat**, it is often used in traditional cooking to spice stews and chutneys. The name preserves both **regional identity and fiery reputation**, reflecting its status as a historic and formidable chili ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/naga-morich/)).
shortDescription: Extremely hot chili from northeastern India and Bangladesh with fruity, slightly smoky flavor.

name: Infinity Pepper
description: **Infinity Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a super‑hot chili pepper developed in the UK by breeder Nick Woods of Fire Foods in Grantham, England. It registers around **1,067,286 to 1,250,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)** and features bulbous, wrinkled pods that ripen from green to bright red. Its flavour has a short fruity prelude that is quickly overwhelmed by a severe delayed‑burn effect, making it a favourite among enthusiasts of extreme heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/infinity-pepper/), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/infinity-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Infinity Pepper”** reflects both its **boundary‑pushing heat** and its experimental origins. The term “Infinity” was chosen to suggest the unimaginable or limitless intensity of its spiciness. It was bred by Nick Woods after a spontaneous greenhouse hybridisation, and for a brief time in 2011 it held the world record for the hottest chili pepper. The name signals a blend of ambition and extremity—a chili that goes beyond conventional limits. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infinity_chili))
shortDescription: UK-bred super-hot chili with a fruity prelude and intense delayed burn.

name: Ghost Pepper
description: **Ghost Pepper** (*Bhut Jolokia*, *Capsicum chinense*) is an **ultra-hot chili pepper** from **northeastern India (Assam, Nagaland, Manipur)**, with heat ranging from **800,000 to over 1,000,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces small, wrinkled red pods with a **fruity, slightly smoky flavor** beneath its extreme heat. It is used in **hot sauces, powders, and spicy dishes** and is recognized worldwide as a super-hot chili ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_pepper), [ChiliMafia](https://www.chilimafia.com/en/chilli/ghost-pepper-bhut-jolokia)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Ghost Pepper”** comes from the Assamese term *Bhut Jolokia*, meaning **“ghost chili”**, a reference to its **potent heat that sneaks up like a ghost** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ghost_pepper)). Traditionally used in northeast Indian cuisine, it is valued for both **culinary and cultural significance**. The English nickname highlights its **elusive, creeping spiciness**, which helped it gain international fame and recognition among super-hot chili enthusiasts worldwide ([ChiliMafia](https://www.chilimafia.com/en/chilli/ghost-pepper-bhut-jolokia)).
shortDescription: Ultra-hot chili from northeastern India with fruity, smoky flavor and intense heat.

name: 7 Pot Brown
description: **7 Pot Brown Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a super‑hot, chocolate‑brown chili pepper native to Trinidad. Its pods are heavily wrinkled, about 2–3 cm long, ripening from lime‑green to dark brown. With heat levels often exceeding **900,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it offers a fruity, nutty undertone before unleashing a fierce burn, making it ideal for hot sauces, dried chili powders, or extreme‑heat culinary use. ([Chili-Shop24](https://www.chili-shop24.de/chili-zucht/chili-samen/schaerfegrad-10/12008/7-pot-brown-chili-samen))
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Brown”** comes from the famous “7 Pot” prefix used in Trinidad, suggesting **one pepper is enough to spice seven pots of stew**. “Brown” refers to the unique chocolate‑brown colour of this variant, distinguishing it from red or yellow 7 Pot cultivars. The pepper’s heritage traces back to Trinidadian farms where growers selected for both extreme heat and the brown hue. Today, its name blends **legendary spiciness and distinctive appearance**, offering both cultural significance and dramatic flavour. ([Scovilla](https://www.scovilla.com/en/7-Pot-Brown-Chili-Samen/900031))
shortDescription: Chocolate-brown Trinidad pepper with fruity, nutty undertones and intense heat.

name: Trinidad Scorpion "Butch-T"
description: **Trinidad Scorpion ‘Butch T’** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili pepper famed for its blistering heat—measuring up to around **1.46 million Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It bears vivid red, heavily wrinkled pods with a distinctive stinger‑like tip, and delivers a short fruity flavour followed by an immediate, intense burn. Originally released around 2011, it remains a prized variety among extreme‑hot pepper enthusiasts and sauce creators ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/trinidad-scorpion-butch-t-chili-pepper/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-butch-t/)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Butch T” pays tribute to **Butch Taylor**, the U.S. hot‑sauce maker and pepper breeder who helped propagate the variety, while “Trinidad Scorpion” references its Trinidadian super‑hot pepper lineage and the pod’s **sting‑like tail** that resembles a scorpion’s barb. The pepper briefly held the title of **world’s hottest chili** in 2011, adding legend to its name. Its origin story encompasses Trinidad, Mississippi and Australia—making it a global icon of extreme heat ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/trinidad-scorpion-butch-t/)).
shortDescription: Ultra-hot Trinidad scorpion pepper with fruity, smoky notes and powerful, lingering burn.

name: 7 Pot Jonah
description: **7 Pot Jonah Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a **super‑hot chili cultivar** from Trinidad, typically ranging between **800,000 and 1,200,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It features bold, wrinkled pods that transition from green to vibrant red, with a pronounced **fruity and tropical flavor**. Its size is somewhat larger than many 7 Pot variants, making it especially prized for **hot sauces, salsas**, or any dish requiring serious heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-jonah/), [SuperHotChiles](https://www.superhotchiles.com/product/trinidad-7-pot-jonah/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“7 Pot Jonah”** merges a legendary heat descriptor and a personal touch. The “7 Pot” prefix alludes to the myth that one pepper can spice **seven pots of stew**—a nod to extreme potency. “Jonah” is believed to reference an early breeder or distributor who popularized this strain, distinguishing it among 7 Pot family cultivars. The name thus captures both the pepper’s **myth‑level heat** and its **distinct identity** within the fiery world of super‑hots.
shortDescription: Trinidadian super-hot pepper with fruity, tropical flavor and intense heat.

name: Dundicut
description: **Dundicut Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, round chili pepper, typically ½‑1 inch in diameter, with dark red pods. It originates from Pakistan’s Sindh region and ripens to a rich ruby hue. Measuring around **55,000–65,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it offers a full‑bodied flavour—fruity, slightly smoky, and robust—while providing moderate heat, perfect for drying, crushing, or spicing dishes. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/dundicut-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Dundicut” (also spelled “Dundicutt” or “Dundike”) comes from its local name in the Sindh province of Pakistan. It’s sometimes called *“gol lal mirch”* in local languages, meaning “round red pepper”. The pepper has been grown traditionally in the arid Thar region for generations, often sun‑dried and stored for winter use. Fun fact: a single crushed Dundicut can flavour two cooking pots, highlighting its concentrated flavour and moderate heat punch. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dundicut))
shortDescription: Small, round Pakistani chili with fruity, smoky flavor and moderate heat.

name: Pequin
description: **Pequin Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum var. glabriusculum*) is a small yet fiery chili pepper from Mexico, typically rating **30,000–60,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Its tiny pods are about 2–4 cm long, ripening from green to vibrant red, and deliver a **citrusy, nutty, and smoky** flavour. Often used in salsas, sauces, and dried for seasoning, they pack a flavourful punch despite their size ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/chili-pequin-chili-peppers/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pequin_pepper)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Pequin” likely stems from the Spanish word *pequeño*, meaning **“small”**, a nod to the pepper’s diminutive size. It is known by many regional names such as *chile petín*, *bird pepper*, and *mashito*, among others, across Mexico and Central America. This pepper grows wild in Mexico’s forests and has been harvested for centuries. A fun fact: it’s often so spicy that **a few peppers can season an entire pot of stew**, exactly like its “7 Pot” cousins emphasise—just in miniature form. ([Sonoran Spice Guide](https://www.sonoranspice.com/blogs/sonoran-spice-product-guides/pequin-peppers), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pequin_pepper)).
shortDescription: Small Mexican chili with citrusy, nutty, and smoky flavor and moderate heat.

name: Tabasco
description: **Tabasco Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a small, slender chili pepper, measuring 2–3 cm in length, native to **Mexico and Central America**. It ripens from green to bright red, delivering a **sharp, tangy, and moderately hot flavor** of around **30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It is the primary ingredient in the famous **Tabasco® hot sauce**, prized worldwide for its vibrant heat and distinct taste ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/tabasco-chili-peppers/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tabasco_pepper)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Tabasco”** comes from the Mexican state of **Tabasco**, where the pepper was first cultivated extensively. It became famous globally when **Edmund McIlhenny** used it in 1868 to produce the original Tabasco® hot sauce. Fun fact: the pepper’s **high capsaicin content** and small size make it perfect for sauces that can last for decades without losing flavor. Its name now signifies both **heritage and iconic heat** ([Tabasco® Official](https://www.tabasco.com/our-story/)).
shortDescription: Small, slender Mexican chili with sharp, tangy flavor and moderate heat.

name: Aurora
description: **Aurora Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, upright ornamental chili pepper that dazzles with a **rainbow of colors**—pods ripen from green to lavender, deep purple, orange, and finally red. With a heat level of approximately **30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it delivers a respectable spicy kick comparable to a cayenne, though its true appeal is visual. Native to Mexico and popular as a decorative houseplant or garden border, it produces compact pods typically about 1.5–2 cm long. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/aurora-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Aurora” refers to the **Aurora Borealis (Northern Lights)**, mirroring the pepper’s transition through multiple vibrant colours simultaneously on a single plant. A fun fact: although primarily used for its ornamental beauty, the Aurora pepper is fully edible and is often used in colourful salsas or dried for spicy flakes. Its petite, bushy habit makes it one of the most popular “living décor” peppers for patios and windowsills, proving that utility and beauty can coexist in one spicy package. ([Garden.org](https://garden.org/plants/view/111818/Pepper-Capsicum-annuum-Aurora/))
shortDescription: Upright ornamental Mexican chili with rainbow colors and moderate heat.

name: Aji Amarillo
description: **Aji Amarillo** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a **vibrant yellow-orange chili pepper** native to Peru, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **30,000 to 50,000 SHU**. It produces medium-sized pods with a **fruity, berry-like flavor** and moderate heat. A staple in Peruvian cuisine, it is used in **sauces, stews, and traditional dishes** like *Papa a la Huancaína* ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_amarillo), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/aji-peppers/aji-amarillo/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Aji Amarillo”** means **“yellow chili”** in Spanish, highlighting its **distinctive bright color** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_amarillo)). It is historically significant, with records of its use dating back to ancient Andean civilizations. Known for its **unique combination of sweetness and heat**, it is often considered the most important ingredient in Peruvian cooking. The name reflects both its **vibrant appearance and essential culinary role** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/aji-peppers/aji-amarillo/)).
shortDescription: Vibrant yellow-orange Peruvian chili with fruity, berry-like flavor and moderate heat.

name: Black Cobra
description: **Black Cobra Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a unique chili originating from Venezuela, featuring pods that grow vertically and transition from jet-black to bright red. Rated between **20,000 and 40,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it offers a heat profile similar to a serrano but with a sharper, more immediate burn. The plant itself is ornamental, with fuzzy silver-green leaves and white flowers, making it a favorite for both garden display and culinary use. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/black-cobra-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Black Cobra” derives from its striking **black coloration** during the early stages of ripening and its “striking” heat level, which mimics the danger of the venomous snake. Also known as *Goat’s Weed*, this variety is prized for its ability to produce fruit even in cooler conditions compared to other chilies. Fun fact: the pods are often dried and crushed into flakes, providing a smoky, earthy base to spice blends while maintaining a respectable level of heat. ([Chili-Shop24](https://www.chili-shop24.de/chili-zucht/chili-samen/schaerfegrad-7-8/12046/black-cobra-chili-samen))
shortDescription: Unique Venezuelan chili with black pods ripening to red and moderate heat.

name: Jwala Finger Hot
description: **Jwala Finger Hot Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a popular Indian chili producing long, slender pods that typically grow to 4–6 inches (10–15 cm) in length. With a heat level around **20,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it delivers a sharp, spicy bite and is commonly used fresh in curries and chutneys or dried for seasoning. The peppers mature from light green to a vibrant red, making them a staple in Indian markets. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/jwala-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Jwala” means **“flame”** or **“intense heat”** in Sanskrit, reflecting the pepper’s status as a quintessential source of spice in Indian cuisine. It is particularly associated with the state of Gujarat, where it is used extensively in local dishes. Fun fact: despite its name suggesting extreme fire, it is considered a moderate heat chili by enthusiasts but is the “go-to” pepper for traditional spicy Indian cooking. Its finger-like shape also makes it easy to dice or use whole for infusing oils. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Jwala_Chile_Peppers_11438.php))
shortDescription: Popular Indian chili with long, slender pods and moderate heat, staple in curries.

name: Japones
description: **Japones Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, thin-walled chili pepper typically 2–3 inches long, ripening from green to a glossy bright red. With a heat range of approximately **15,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it delivers a clean, sharp heat without being overpowering. Its thin skin makes it exceptionally well-suited for drying whole, where it becomes a common sight in spice markets and Asian-inspired culinary spice blends. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/japones-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: Despite its name, the Japones pepper is widely grown across **Mexico and China** and is a mainstay in many “Szechuan‑style” dishes and Mexican salsas alike. The term “Japones” (Spanish for “Japanese”) hints at its frequent use in Japanese and Pan‑Asian cuisine, though it likely originated in the Americas. A fun fact: because they are so easy to dry and store, they are often the “secret ingredient” providing consistent heat in commercial crushed red pepper flakes. ([Sonoran Spice](https://www.sonoranspice.com/blogs/sonoran-spice-product-guides/japones-peppers))
shortDescription: Slender, thin-walled Japanese chili with clean, sharp heat, ideal for drying.

name: Lemon Drop
description: **Lemon Drop Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*), also known as *Ají Limo*, is a bright yellow, citrus‑flavoured chili from Peru. It measures roughly 2–3 inches long and registers **15,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pepper has a distinct **lemony, fruity aroma** that sets it apart from other chilies, making it a favourite for fresh salsas, seafood dishes, and hot sauces. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/aji-peppers/lemon-drop-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Lemon Drop” is a direct reference to its vibrant yellow colour and unique citrusy flavour profile. In its native Peru, it’s traditionally used in **ceviche** to provide both heat and a complementary fruitiness. Fun fact: the plants are prolific growers and can produce over 100 pods in a single season. Its name perfectly captures the essence of a pepper that delivers a “drop” of citrus sunshine alongside a respectable spicy kick. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/lemon-drop-pepper/))
shortDescription: Bright yellow Peruvian chili with lemony flavor and moderate heat, ideal for seafood.

name: Black Pearl
description: **Black Pearl Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a striking ornamental chili developed by the USDA’s Agricultural Research Service. It produces round, marble-like pods that ripen from a glossy jet-black to a vivid crimson red. Reaching **10,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it offers a heat similar to a serrano but with a slightly more bitter, citrusy undertone. The plant’s foliage is also uniquely dark, often appearing deep purple or black in full sun. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/black-pearl-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Black Pearl” was chosen for the pepper’s **black, pearl-like shape** and its jewel-like appearance in a garden setting. Developed as an award-winning ornamental variety (AAS Winner 2006), it is prized for its ability to add dramatic colour to landscapes and containers. Fun fact: while widely grown for its beauty, the peppers are completely edible and can add both colour and heat to unique salsas. Its name emphasizes its rare, dark elegance among more traditional green and red chili varieties. ([Garden.org](https://garden.org/plants/view/111818/Pepper-Capsicum-annuum-Black-Pearl/))
shortDescription: Striking ornamental US chili with black pearl-like pods and moderate heat.

name: Manzano
description: **Manzano Pepper** (*Capsicum pubescens*) is a unique chili originating from the Andes, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **12,000 to 30,000 SHU**. It produces round, apple-shaped pods with **thick walls and black seeds**, a hallmark of its species. Known for its **fruity, citrusy flavor** and juicy texture, it is used in **salsas, sauces, and fresh applications** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manzano_pepper), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/manzano-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Manzano”** means **“apple”** in Spanish, referencing its **rounded shape** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manzano_pepper)). It belongs to the *Capsicum pubescens* species, which is distinguished by **hairy leaves and black seeds**. Unlike many other chilies, it thrives in **cooler climates and higher altitudes**. Its name reflects both its **distinctive appearance and unique botanical characteristics** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/manzano-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Unique South American chili with apple-shaped pods, black seeds, and fruity flavor.

name: Chile de Arbol
description: **Chile de Árbol** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, thin-walled chili pepper typically 2–3 inches long, native to Mexico. It is rated between **15,000 and 30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, delivering a clean, sharp heat with **smoky and nutty** undertones. It matures from green to bright red and is most commonly used in its dried form for salsas, sauces, and decorative wreaths (ristras) due to its vibrant color. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/chile-de-arbol-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Chile de Árbol” means **“tree chili”** in Spanish, a reference to its thick, woody stem and upright growth habit that resembles a small tree. It is a staple in Mexican cuisine, often used to create fiery red salsas and infused oils. Fun fact: despite being much hotter than a jalapeño, it is valued for its complex flavor rather than just its burn. Its name captures both its **sturdy plant structure and its essential role** in traditional spice markets. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chile_de_arbol))
shortDescription: Slender Mexican chili with smoky and nutty flavor and moderate heat.

name: Bishop's Crown
description: **Bishop's Crown Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a uniquely shaped chili from South America, typically rating **5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Its pods have three distinct wings, resembling a **bishop's mitre or crown**, and ripen from green to bright red. The flavor is notably **sweet and fruity**, with heat concentrated primarily in the core and seeds, making it a versatile pepper for mild to medium dishes. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bishops-crown-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bishop’s Crown” is derived from the pod’s **distinctive three-pointed shape** which looks like a religious head covering. Also known as *Christmas Bell* or *Joker's Hat*, this variety is prized for its high yield and unusual appearance. Fun fact: the wings of the pepper can be completely sweet, while the center packs a punch, providing a unique “surprise” with every bite. Its name captures both its regal appearance and its popularity as an ornamental-culinary hybrid. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Bishops_Crown_Chile_Peppers_11438.php))
shortDescription: Uniquely shaped South American chili with sweet and fruity flavor and moderate heat.

name: Hinkelhatz
description: **Hinkelhatz Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an heirloom chili traditionally grown by the Pennsylvania Dutch since the late 1800s. It produces small, heart-shaped pods about 1 inch long that range from **15,000 to 30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The flavor is sharp and tangy, often used in pickling or to create traditional “pepper vinegar.” It is highly resistant to pests and matures into vibrant red or orange colors. ([Seed Savers Exchange](https://www.seedsavers.org/hinkelhatz-orange-pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Hinkelhatz” translates to **“chicken heart”** in Pennsylvania Dutch, accurately describing the pepper’s petite, heart-like shape. This variety is one of the oldest heritage peppers in North America and was nearly lost before being rediscovered by seed savers. Fun fact: it’s a favorite for making traditional sauerkraut-based relishes. Its name pays homage to both its **unique form and its deep cultural roots** in American agricultural history. ([PepperGeek](https://peppergeek.com/hinkelhatz-pepper/))
shortDescription: Heirloom US chili with heart-shaped pods and sharp, tangy flavor, ideal for pickling.

name: Black Prince
description: **Black Prince Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an ornamental chili prized for its dark purple, nearly black foliage and pods. Reaching roughly **5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it delivers a mild to moderate heat. The small, upright pods ripen from deep black to bright red, creating a stunning visual contrast against the dark leaves. It is a compact plant, ideal for containers and decorative garden borders. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/black-prince-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Black Prince” reflects its **dark, regal coloration** and petite, “princely” stature. While primarily grown for its beauty, the peppers are edible and offer a flavor similar to a mild cayenne. Fun fact: the black pods only transition to red at the very end of their growth cycle, allowing the plant to maintain its “gothic” appearance for most of the season. Its name emphasizes its **rare, dark aesthetic and sophisticated garden presence**. ([Garden.org](https://garden.org/plants/view/111818/Pepper-Capsicum-annuum-Black-Prince/))
shortDescription: Ornamental chili with dark purple-black foliage and moderate heat.

name: Count Dracula
description: **[Count Dracula Pepper]()** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a striking ornamental chili sought for its dramatic appearance. It typically ranges from **5,000 to 30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, delivering a mild to moderate burn. The plant features black‑purple foliage, deep purple flowers, and pods that ripen from jet‑black to vivid red—ideal for containers and Halloween‑themed gardens. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/count-dracula-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Count Dracula”** was chosen for its gothic aesthetic and vampiric appeal: the chili’s **dark leaves and pods** bring to mind the legendary Transylvanian vampire. The “Count” reference matches the regal title and the pepper’s dramatic presentation, while the “Dracula” element evokes mystery and night‑time intrigue. Fun fact: the pods are edible, although grown primarily for ornamentation, and the cultivar makes a bold visual statement alongside its modest heat. ([The Hippy Seed Company](https://thehippyseedcompany.com/product/count-dracula-chilli/))
shortDescription: Striking ornamental chili with glossy black-purple foliage and moderate heat.

name: Bulgarian Carrot
description: This striking heirloom pepper from Bulgaria features bright orange, carrot‑shaped pods about 3 to 4 inches long, ripening from green to vivid orange. With a heat range of approximately 5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units, it sits in the medium‑hot category and delivers a sweet‐fruity flavor with a crisp texture, ideal for roasting, pickling or salsas. Its crunchy skin and vibrant color make it as decorative as it is edible. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bulgarian-carrot-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bulgarian Carrot” owes both to its country of origin—Bulgaria—and its resemblance to a carrot in shape and bright orange color. Known locally as *Shipka* or *Shifka*, the pepper was cultivated near Bulgaria’s Black Sea region and reportedly smuggled out of the Iron Curtain era in the 1980s. Its crunchy flesh makes it exceptional for roasting and pickling, while its Eastern European heritage and striking appearance give it a compelling back‑story. ([NatureWord](https://www.natureword.com/what-are-bulgarian-carrot-peppers/))
shortDescription: Striking Bulgarian pepper with sweet-fruity flavor and medium heat.

name: Fish Pepper
description: **Fish Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a versatile heirloom chili producing small pods that ripen through hues of white with green stripes, orange, brown, and red, and deliver medium heat — roughly **5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The plants also display striking green‑and‑white variegated foliage and are a favourite for both culinary use and ornamental value. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fish_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Fish Pepper” is rooted in the pepper’s historical use in **Baltimore and Philadelphia seafood houses** during the late 19th and early 20th centuries—particularly in crab, oyster, and fish dishes in African‑American communities. ([Epicurious article](https://www.epicurious.com/ingredients/fish-peppers-african-american-garden-article)) A fun fact: some immature pods remain pale white, making them ideal for spicing white sauces without altering the colour. The cultivar nearly vanished mid‑20th century but was revived thanks to seed‑saving efforts, making it both a culinary gem and a piece of cultural heritage.
shortDescription: Heirloom chili with variegated foliage and moderate heat, ideal for seafood.

name: Sport
description: **Sport Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, medium‑hot chili typically used in its pickled form. It registers between **10,000 and 23,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, delivering a sharp, vinegary punch. The pods are usually about 1–2 inches long and ripen to bright red, though they are most famous for being harvested while green for use in iconic American dishes. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sport_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Sport” is believed to refer to its popularity as a **condiment at sporting events**, most notably as an essential topping for a **Chicago‑style hot dog**. Fun fact: the “sport” name might also derive from it being a genetic “sport” (a natural mutation) from other chili varieties. These peppers are almost exclusively consumed pickled in a salty brine, providing a crisp texture and tangy heat that cuts through rich meats and cheeses. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/sport-peppers/))
shortDescription: Small, medium-hot US chili with sharp flavor, iconic topping for Chicago-style hot dogs.

name: Peter Pepper
description: **Peter Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an heirloom chili variety famed for its **unusually contorted shape**, often described as phallic. It carries a heat level of **10,000–23,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, similar to a serrano, with a spicy, pungent flavor. The pods grow to 3–4 inches long and ripen from green to bright red or orange, thriving in warm climates. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Peter Pepper” is a playful and slightly euphemistic reference to the **distinctive, anatomical shape** of its fruit. Also known as *Chilly Willy*, it is grown primarily as a novelty and conversation piece in home gardens. Fun fact: despite its bizarre appearance, it is a versatile pepper for making hot sauces or drying into powder. Its name captures the pepper’s unique place as the most “Instagrammable” and famous novelty chili in the world. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/peter-pepper/))
shortDescription: Heirloom US chili with unusually contorted pods and moderate heat, often grown as a novelty.

name: Hungarian Wax
description: **Hungarian Wax Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a medium-sized chili with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **5,000 to 10,000 SHU**. It produces smooth, yellow pods with a **tangy, sweet flavor** and moderate heat. Commonly used in **pickling, frying, and roasting**, it is a staple in Central European cuisine ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hungarian_wax_pepper), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/hungarian-wax-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Hungarian Wax”** reflects its **origin and waxy skin texture** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hungarian_wax_pepper)). It is often confused with the Banana Pepper due to its similar appearance, but it is **significantly hotter**. Known for its **versatility in the kitchen**, it is prized for its ability to hold its shape when cooked. The name reflects both its **distinctive appearance and cultural heritage** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/hungarian-wax-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Medium-sized Hungarian chili with tangy, sweet flavor and moderate heat, ideal for pickling.

name: Aleppo
description: **Aleppo Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*), also known as the *Halaby pepper*, is a medium-heat chili famous for its use in Middle Eastern cuisine. It typically registers around **10,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pepper has a rich, **complex flavor profile** that is salty, fruity (reminiscent of raisins), and slightly smoky. It is most commonly found as dried, coarsely ground flakes that add a gentle warmth and deep fragrance to meat, vegetables, and dips. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleppo_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Aleppo” comes from the **city of Aleppo in Syria**, a historic hub along the Silk Road where this pepper has been cultivated for centuries. Fun fact: unlike many other crushed peppers, Aleppo flakes are often processed with salt and oil to preserve their moisture and vivid red colour. Its name signifies both its **geographic heritage and its legendary status** as one of the world’s most gourmet finishing spices. ([SpiceLibrary](https://spicelibrary.com.au/product/aleppo-pepper/))
shortDescription: Medium-heat Syrian chili with complex, salty, fruity flavor, ideal for Middle Eastern cuisine.

name: Fresno
description: **Fresno Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a medium-sized chili with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **2,500 to 10,000 SHU**. It produces smooth, red pods with a **smoky, fruity flavor** and mild to moderate heat. Often used in **salsas, sauces, and roasting**, it is a popular alternative to the Jalapeño ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fresno_chili), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/fresno-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Fresno”** reflects its **origin in Fresno, California**, where it was first cultivated in 1952 ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fresno_chili)). It matures from green to red, with the red pods offering a **sweeter and more complex flavor**. Known for its **thin walls and crisp texture**, it is prized for its culinary versatility. Its name reflects both its **geographic birthplace and its role as a staple in California chili culture** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/fresno-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Medium-sized US chili with smoky, fruity flavor and mild to moderate heat, popular in salsas.

name: Hatch
description: **Hatch Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a term used for varieties grown in the Hatch Valley of New Mexico, with heat ranging from **1,000 to 8,000 SHU**. It produces large, elongated pods with a **rich, earthy flavor** and mild to moderate heat. A cultural icon in New Mexico, it is used in **roasted green chili sauces, stews, and burgers** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Mexico_chili), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hatch-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Hatch”** refers to the **Hatch Valley in New Mexico**, the specific region where these peppers must be grown to carry the name ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_Mexico_chili)). The region’s unique soil and climate contribute to the pepper’s **distinctive taste and culinary prestige**. Known for its **annual roasting festivals**, it is deeply embedded in Southwestern culture. Its name reflects both its **geographic origin and its status as a premium chili variety** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hatch-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Cultural icon of New Mexico with rich, earthy flavor and mild to moderate heat.

name: Puya
description: **Puya Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, dried chili from Mexico that is often considered the hotter relative of the Guajillo. It registers between **5,000 and 8,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. With a **fruity, berry‑like flavour** and a more intense heat than many other dried peppers, it is used to add depth and a sharp kick to salsas, moles, and enchilada sauces. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/puya-chili-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Puya” means **“spike”** or **“prod”** in Spanish, referring to its long, thin, and pointed shape. It is a key ingredient in Central Mexican cuisine, valued for its ability to balance sweetness with a respectable burn. Fun fact: while it looks very similar to a Guajillo, the Puya is smaller and significantly spicier. Its name reflects its **pointed appearance and its “sharp” impact** on a dish’s flavour profile. ([Sonoran Spice](https://www.sonoranspice.com/blogs/sonoran-spice-product-guides/puya-peppers))
shortDescription: Slender Mexican chili with fruity, berry-like flavor and moderate heat, relative of the Guajillo.

name: Chipotle
description: **Chipotle Pepper** is a smoke-dried Jalapeño, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **2,500 to 8,000 SHU**. It produces a **rich, smoky, and earthy flavor** with moderate heat. A staple in Mexican and Tex-Mex cuisines, it is used in **salsas, marinades, and stews** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chipotle), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/chipotle-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Chipotle”** comes from the Nahuatl word *chīlpoctli*, meaning **“smoked chili”** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chipotle)). The process of smoking the peppers was developed by ancient Mesoamericans as a way to preserve them. Known for its **deep, complex flavor profile**, it is one of the most popular and recognizable ingredients in modern Mexican cooking. Its name reflects both its **historic preservation method and its essential culinary role** ([National Geographic](https://www.nationalgeographic.com/culture/article/chipotle)).
shortDescription: Smoked and dried jalapeño with smoky, earthy flavor and medium heat.

name: Espelette
description: **Espelette Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*, variety Gorria) is a mild‑heat chili pepper from the Basque region of France. It has a Scoville rating around 1,000‑4,000 SHU, and offers a flavour described as **sweet, fruity, and subtly smoky**, making it ideal for finishing dishes or seasoning traditional Basque recipes. It’s typically harvested late summer, sun‑dried and ground into a vibrant red powder that retains its fragrance and colour. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Espelette_pepper), [SpiceLibrary](https://spicelibrary.org/spice/pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Espelette” comes from the **village of Espelette** in the Pyrénées‑Atlantiques department of France, where the pepper has been cultivated since the 16th century. It is the only French pepper to hold an **Appellation d'Origine Protégée (AOP)** status, meaning true Espelette must be grown in this specific region. Fun fact: every October, the village hosts a festival celebrating the harvest, where strings of peppers (cordes) hang from almost every balcony. ([DiscoverFrance](https://www.discoverfrance.net/France/Basque/Espelette.shtml))
shortDescription: Mild French chili with sweet, fruity, and smoky flavor, staple in Basque cuisine.

name: Cherry Bomb
description: **Cherry Bomb Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, round chili pepper with thick walls, measuring about 1–2 inches in diameter. It offers a mild to moderate heat of **2,500–5,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, similar to a jalapeño. The pods are prized for their **sweet and juicy flavour**, making them a top choice for pickling and stuffing with cheese. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/cherry-bomb-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cherry Bomb” is a direct reference to its **bright red, cherry‑like appearance** and its “explosive” (though manageable) heat level. Because of their thick flesh, they are remarkably durable and hold up well to grilling. Fun fact: they are often the first peppers to be picked in a garden because their shape and color make them so inviting. Its name perfectly encapsulates the pepper’s **visual appeal and its punchy, festive character**. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/cherry-bomb-peppers/))
shortDescription: Small, round US chili with sweet and juicy flavor and mild to moderate heat, ideal for pickling.

name: Guajillo
description: **Guajillo Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a large, dried chili with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **2,500 to 5,000 SHU**. It produces smooth, dark red pods with a **sweet, smoky flavor** and mild heat. A staple in Mexican cuisine, it is used in **moles, sauces, and stews** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guajillo_chili), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/guajillo-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Guajillo”** comes from the Nahuatl word for **“little gourd”**, referencing its **smooth, leathery skin** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guajillo_chili)). It is one of the “holy trinity” of Mexican dried chilies, alongside Ancho and Pasilla. Known for its **versatility and deep flavor**, it is often toasted before being rehydrated to enhance its smokiness. Its name reflects both its **distinctive appearance and its essential culinary status** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/guajillo-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Large Mexican dried chili with sweet, smoky flavor and mild heat, staple in moles.

name: Numex Big Jim
description: **NuMex Big Jim Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an exceptionally large New Mexico-style chili, with Scoville Heat Units typically ranging from **500 to 3,000 SHU**. It produces giant, thick-walled pods that can reach over 12 inches in length. Known for its **mild to medium heat and sweet, earthy flavor**, it is the premier variety for **roasting, stuffing (Chiles Rellenos), and making green chili sauce**. ([New Mexico State University](https://cpi.nmsu.edu/chile-info/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Big Jim” honors **Jim Lytle**, a New Mexico farmer and chili researcher who helped develop the variety in the 1970s. It currently holds the Guinness World Record for the **longest chili pepper ever grown**. Fun fact: because of its massive size, one single pepper can often serve as a full meal when stuffed. Its name emphasizes its **unprecedented size and its deep connection** to the agricultural heritage of the Hatch Valley. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/nu-mex-big-jim/))
shortDescription: Exceptionally large US chili with mild to medium heat, ideal for roasting and stuffing.

name: Cascabel
description: **Cascabel Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, round chili pepper about 1–2 inches in diameter that ripens from green to dark red‑brown. With a mild to medium heat range of approximately **1,500–3,000 Scoville Heat Units**, it delivers a nutty, earthy flavor with mild smokiness. Commonly used dried in Mexican sauces, stews, and salsas, its smooth skin and versatile heat make it ideal for layering rich flavours without overwhelming heat. ([CooksInfo](https://www.cooksinfo.com/cascabel-peppers), [SpiceLibrary](https://spicelibrary.org/spice/chiles-cascabel))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cascabel” comes from the Spanish word for **“little bell”** or **“rattle”**, reflecting the characteristic **rattling sound** the seeds make inside the dried pod. Also known as *chile bola* (“ball chili”), this cultivar is native to Mexico and has been grown in states such as Coahuila, Durango and Jalisco for generations. Fun fact: it’s one of the few dried chiles whose shape remains spherical after drying, allowing the seeds to rattle freely—hence its musical nickname and unique sensory appeal. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cascabel_chili))
shortDescription: Small, round Mexican chili with nutty, earthy flavor and mild to medium heat.

name: Alma Paprika
description: **Alma Paprika Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a Hungarian heirloom cultivated primarily for drying into paprika spice. The pods are squat, 7–10 cm long and 5–7 cm wide, with thick flesh that ripens from white to orange to red. They have a mild to moderate heat level (around 1,000–3,000 SHU) and a sweet, crisp flavor, making them perfect fresh, pickled, or dried into vibrant paprika powder. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Alma_Paprika_Chile_Peppers_13711.php), [PepperJoe](https://pepperjoe.com/products/alma-paprika))
aditionalInfo: The name **“Alma”** means **“apple”** in Hungarian, reflecting the pepper’s rounded, apple‑like shape and substantial body. Originating in Hungary, the variety became essential in the region’s paprika production—especially in the Danube valley homes where peppers were sun‑dried for spice. Fun fact: although mild for chili fans, Alma Paprika’s thick walls and abundant flesh make it ideal for stuffing or grilling, and its powder is a kitchen staple in Hungarian dishes like goulash. ([Tomatofifou](https://www.tomatofifou.com/en/produit/alma-paprika/), [Kokopelli](https://kokopelli-semences.com/en/p/alma-paprika-fort-mid-season-pepper))
shortDescription: Mild to moderate Hungarian chili with sweet, crisp flavor and ideal for drying into paprika.

name: Italian Long Hot
description: **Italian Long Hot Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an elongated, tapered chili pepper typically 6–8 inches (15–20 cm) long with thin walls and lower heat—often around **100–1,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, though heat can vary. It starts green and ripens to vivid red, offering a sweet, tangy flavor with mild spiciness, making it ideal for frying, roasting, or topping onto sandwiches and pizzas. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/italian-long-hot-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))
aditionalInfo: The name “Italian Long Hot” highlights both its origin and shape: “Italian” refers to its roots in Italy and “Long Hot” describes its elongated form and mild heat. This pepper gained popularity especially in Italian‑American cuisine in regions like Philadelphia and New York. A fun fact: despite the “hot” in its name, it often has less heat than a jalapeño, making it a “gateway” spice. It’s also commonly roasted in olive oil and used as a flavorful topping rather than a fiery filler. ([Spicy Exchange](https://spicyexchange.com/peppers/italian-long-hots/?utm_source=chatgpt.com))
shortDescription: Elongated Italian chili with sweet, tangy flavor and mild spiciness.

name: Cubanelle
description: **Cubanelle Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild sweet pepper typically measuring 4–6 inches long with thin walls and a tapered shape. Its heat level ranges from about **100 to 1,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it much milder than a jalapeño. The flesh is sweet and slightly fruity, and is prized for stuffing, sautéing, or using raw in salads and sandwiches. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/cubanelle-chili-peppers/), [PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/cubanelle-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name *“Cubanelle”* derives from its roots in Caribbean and Latin American cuisine—especially Cuba, where the variety is known as *ají cubanela*. It later gained popularity in Italian cooking under the nickname “Italian frying pepper.” Fun fact: because of its thin walls, Cubanelle peppers fry quickly and develop a rich, sweet flavor; they are a traditional component in *sofrito* bases and feature in stuffed‑pepper dishes across the Dominican Republic, Puerto Rico, and Italian kitchens alike. ([The Spruce Eats](https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-cubanelle-peppers-4776455), [Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Cubanelle_Italian_Green_Chile_Peppers_9955.php))
shortDescription: Mild sweet pepper with thin walls and a tapered shape, ideal for stuffing and sautéing.

name: Peppadew
description: **Peppadew Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum ‘Juanita’*) is a sweet‑and‑tangy chili pepper originating from South Africa’s Limpopo province, with a mild heat of around **1,000–1,200 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. These small, bright red to yellow pods are typically pickled and jarred, offering a crisp texture, fruity warmth, and vibrant flavour ideal for antipasti, salads, and stuffed‑pepper snacks ([AllRecipes](https://www.allrecipes.com/article/what-are-peppadew-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
aditionalInfo: The name “Peppadew” is a trademarked brand created by South African company Peppadew International to market the sweet piquanté pepper. It derives from a playful fusion of the words “pepper” and “dew,” evoking the pepper’s sweet freshness and early‑morning harvest glow ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/picuante-peppadew-chili-peppers/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)). Fun fact: discovered in the early 1990s, it quickly gained popularity worldwide—often stuffed with soft cheeses or added to pizzas for its distinctive flavour and color ([The Spruce Eats](https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-peppadews-5212958?utm_source=chatgpt.com)).
shortDescription: Sweet-and-tangy chili with mild heat, ideal for pickling and adding vibrant flavor.

name: Cajun Belle
description: **Cajun Belle Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, blocky sweet pepper with a mild spicy finish, measuring 2–3 inches in length. Rated at roughly **100–1,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, it combines the sweetness of a bell pepper with a hint of Southern warmth. It ripens from green to orange and finally to deep red, thriving in home gardens as a compact and high-yielding variety. ([AAS Winners](https://all-americaselections.org/product/pepper-cajun-belle/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cajun Belle” evokes the **vibrant, spicy culinary tradition of Louisiana** and the pepper’s petite “bell-like” shape. An All-America Selections winner (2010), it is praised for its disease resistance and ornamental appeal. Fun fact: the peppers are often harvested at the orange stage for a balanced flavor or left to turn red for maximum sweetness and heat. Its name reflects its **delicate balance of sweet and spicy**, making it a favorite for “Cajun-style” appetizers and salads. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/cajun-belle-pepper/))
shortDescription: Small, blocky US pepper with sweet flavor and mild spicy finish, ideal for appetizers.

name: Pepperoncini
description: **Pepperoncini** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild, tangy chili pepper with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **100 to 500 SHU**. It produces small, wrinkled pods with a **mildly sweet and acidic flavor**. Commonly used in **pickling, salads, and Italian sandwiches**, it is a staple in Mediterranean and Italian-American cuisines ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peperoncino), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/pepperoncini-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Pepperoncini”** is the plural form of *peperoncino* in Italian, meaning **“small pepper”** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peperoncino)). In the US, it specifically refers to the mild, pickled Greek or Italian varieties. Known for its **crisp texture and low heat**, it is often found as a garnish in pizza boxes and deli salads. Its name reflects both its **diminutive size and its status as a quintessential Italian condiment** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/pepperoncini-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Mild, tangy Italian chili with sweet and acidic flavor, staple in salads and sandwiches.

name: Pimiento
description: **Pimiento Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a small, heart-shaped sweet pepper with very low heat, measuring between **100 and 500 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Known for its thick flesh and sweet, aromatic flavor, it is the primary source of the red stuffing found in green olives. It matures from green to a deep, glossy red and is a key ingredient in many Spanish and Southern American dishes. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pimiento))
aditionalInfo: The name “Pimiento” is the **Spanish word for “pepper”**, derived from the Latin *pigmentum* (pigment), a nod to its vibrant color. Also known as *cherry peppers*, they are famous for their role in “pimiento cheese,” a classic Southern US spread. Fun fact: while most sweet peppers are eaten fresh, pimientos are uniquely suited for canning and pickling due to their durability and moisture content. Its name reflects its **historical role as a natural food coloring** and its essential place in Mediterranean gastronomy. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/pimiento-pepper/))
shortDescription: Small, heart-shaped Spanish pepper with sweet flavor and very low heat, ideal for stuffing olives.

name: Carmen Italian Sweet
description: **Carmen Italian Sweet Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a bull’s-horn style sweet pepper, reaching 6–9 inches in length with absolutely zero heat (**0 SHU**). It offers an exceptionally **sweet, rich flavour** that intensifies as it ripens to a brilliant red. Prize-winning (AAS 2006) for its high yield and early maturity, it is a favourite for grilling, roasting, and eating fresh in salads. ([All-America Selections](https://all-americaselections.org/product/pepper-carmen/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Carmen” is a classic Italian name, chosen to honor its **Italian heritage** and elegant, tapered shape. Unlike standard bell peppers, the Carmen has a wider shoulder and more concentrated sugar content. Fun fact: even when green, it is sweeter than most other peppers, but its full potential is reached at the dark red stage. Its name emphasizes its **premium culinary quality and “sweetheart” status** among gardener-chefs. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/carmen-pepper/))
shortDescription: Bull's-horn style Italian sweet pepper with zero heat, ideal for grilling and roasting.

name: Banana Pepper
description: **Banana Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild sweet pepper with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **0 to 500 SHU**. It produces smooth, yellow pods with a **mildly tangy and sweet flavor**. Commonly used in **pickling, salads, and pizza toppings**, it is a popular mild chili variety ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banana_pepper), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/banana-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Banana Pepper”** reflects its **elongated shape and bright yellow color**, which resemble a banana ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banana_pepper)). It ripens to orange or red if left on the vine, becoming sweeter in the process. Known for its **versatility and mildness**, it is often confused with the hotter Hungarian Wax pepper. Its name reflects both its **distinctive appearance and its popular appeal as a mild condiment** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/banana-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Mild sweet pepper with elongated shape and bright yellow color, popular in pickling and salads.

name: Santa Fe Grande
description: **Santa Fe Grande Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a prolific and hardy chili developed in the US, producing pods roughly 2–3 inches long. It offers a mild to medium heat of **500–700 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, providing a gentle warmth rather than intense spice. The peppers ripen from a pale yellow to orange and finally red, delivering a sweet, slightly smoky flavor that is excellent for pickling and fresh salsas. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/santa-fe-grande-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Santa Fe Grande” reflects its **Southwestern origin** and its “grand” (large/prolific) fruiting habit. Also known as the *Yellow Wax Pepper*, it is prized by commercial growers for its resistance to tobacco mosaic virus. Fun fact: because they transition through many colours simultaneously, a single plant is often grown as an ornamental. Its name captures the **essence of New Mexican chili culture** and its versatility in both the kitchen and the garden. ([Garden.org](https://garden.org/plants/view/111818/Pepper-Capsicum-annuum-Santa-Fe-Grande/))
shortDescription: Prolific and hardy US chili with sweet, slightly smoky flavor and mild heat.

name: Italian Sweet
description: **Italian Sweet Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a generic term for several bull’s-horn (corno di toro) varieties like *Marconi Red*, which have zero heat (**0 SHU**). These peppers grow to about 6–8 inches long and are prized for their thin skins and exceptionally high sugar content. They ripen from green to a vivid, glossy red and provide a deep, sweet flavour that is superior to standard supermarket bell peppers. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/marconi-peppers/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Italian Sweet” refers to its **origin and culinary specialty**—these are the quintessential frying peppers of Italy. They are a staple in Mediterranean diets, often sautéed in olive oil with garlic or used in traditional *peperonata*. Fun fact: their thin walls make them much easier to digest when roasted compared to thick-walled bells. Its name emphasizes its **gentle nature and its heritage** as a foundation of traditional Italian home cooking. ([The Spruce Eats](https://www.thespruceeats.com/what-are-italian-sweet-peppers-4776455))
shortDescription: Bull's-horn style Italian pepper with zero heat and high sugar content, ideal for frying.

name: Melrose
description: **Melrose Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an heirloom frying pepper introduced by Italian immigrants to the Chicago area. It is entirely heat-free (**0 SHU**) and produces 4–6 inch tapered pods that ripen to a brilliant red. The flavor is famously rich and sweet, with thin walls that allow it to blister and soften quickly when sautéed or roasted. ([Seed Savers Exchange](https://www.seedsavers.org/melrose-pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Melrose” honors **Melrose Park, Illinois**, where Italian families famously cultivated this variety for generations in their home gardens. It has become a local legend in the Chicago culinary scene, particularly in Italian-American household recipes. Fun fact: it is considered more flavorful than the standard bell pepper, with many cooks refusing to use any other variety for their stuffed pepper recipes. Its name reflects its **immigrant heritage and its status as a regional American heirloom**. ([Chicago Tribune](https://www.chicagotribune.com/dining/recipes/ct-melrose-peppers-story.html))
shortDescription: Heirloom Italian frying pepper with zero heat and rich, sweet flavor, Chicago regional favorite.

name: Purple Beauty
description: **Purple Beauty Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a stunning bell pepper variety with zero heat (**0 SHU**), producing blocky, thick-walled fruits. Its most notable feature is its deep, royal purple color, which appears while the pepper is still immature and persists for several weeks. The flavor is mild, sweet, and crisp, identical to a high-quality green bell pepper but with a significantly more dramatic presentation. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/purple-beauty-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Purple Beauty” is a direct tribute to its **vibrant, jewel-toned appearance** and its “beautiful” status in a garden or gourmet salad. While it eventually ripens to a dark red if left on the vine, it is almost always harvested at the purple stage for its visual impact. Fun fact: the purple pigment is mostly on the skin, so the flesh inside remains a light green, creating a beautiful contrast when sliced. Its name emphasizes its **role as a visually elite sweet pepper** for modern culinary plating. ([Garden.org](https://garden.org/plants/view/111818/Pepper-Capsicum-annuum-Purple-Beauty/))
