shortDescription: Stunning US bell pepper with zero heat and deep purple color, ideal for salads.

name: 7 Pot Bubblegum
description: **7 Pot Bubblegum Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an exotic super‑hot chili, typically rating around **850,000–1,000,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It is famous for its **unique “bleeding” calyx**, where the red color of the pod extends up the stem. The flavor is surprisingly fruity and sweet (reminiscent of bubblegum) before the extreme heat takes over. It was developed by UK breeder Jon Harper. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-bubblegum/))
aditionalInfo: The name “7 Pot Bubblegum” comes from its **bubblegum-like aroma** and its membership in the legendary Trinidadian “7 Pot” family. Its most distinctive feature, the red-colored stem cap, is a result of a cross between a Moruga Scorpion and a 7 Pot Yellow. Fun fact: it is one of the only peppers in the world where the stem actually changes color to match the fruit. Its name perfectly captures the **unexpected sweetness and bizarre botanical trait** that makes it a favorite for chili collectors. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/7-pot-bubblegum/))
shortDescription: Exotic super-hot UK chili with bubblegum-like flavor and unique bleeding calyx.

name: 7 Pot Primo
description: **7 Pot Primo** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a legendary super‑hot pepper developed by horticulturist Troy Primeaux in Louisiana. It registers at a staggering **1,473,480 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are small, red, and extremely distorted, often featuring a **long, slender “stinger” tail** that rivals the Carolina Reaper. It has a fruity, floral flavor profile that is quickly overtaken by a devastating, immediate heat. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-primo/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Primo” is the **nickname of its creator, Troy Primeaux**, combined with the “7 Pot” lineage. There is a long-standing debate in the chili community about its relationship to the Carolina Reaper, as both share a very similar appearance and heat level. Fun fact: Primeaux developed this variety in 2005 by crossing a Naga Morich with a Trinidad 7 Pot. Its name signifies its **elite status and “premier” (primo) position** among the hottest peppers in existence. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/7-pot-primo/))
shortDescription: Legendary US super-hot pepper with long stinger tail and devastating heat.

name: 7 Pot Yellow
description: **7 Pot Yellow Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a vibrant, golden variant of the traditional Trinidad 7 Pot, measuring roughly **1,000,000–1,200,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It offers a **bright, tropical fruit flavor** with a slightly more floral and citrusy note than its red cousins. The pods are bumpy, wrinkled, and mature to a beautiful sunshine-yellow, maintaining the legendary “7 pot” heat that can spice seven pots of food. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/7-pot-yellow/))
aditionalInfo: The name “7 Pot Yellow” follows the Trinidadian tradition of naming ultra-hots based on their color and power. This variant is often considered **slightly less aggressive** than the red or brown versions, though it is still firmly in the super-hot category. Fun fact: its sunny color and citrusy undertones make it a favorite for “yellow” super-hot sauces containing pineapple or mango. Its name reflects its **vibrant hue and its lethal Caribbean pedigree**. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/7-pot-yellow-chili-peppers/))
shortDescription: Vibrant golden Trinidad pepper with tropical fruit flavor and intense heat.

name: Aji Cachucha
description: **Ají Cachucha Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a small, mild pepper originating from Cuba and the Caribbean. It registers between **500 and 1,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it a rare non-spicy member of the Habanero family. The pods are shaped like small, squashed lanterns and have a **rich, floral, and sweet aroma** without the typical burn. It is a fundamental ingredient in Caribbean sofrito and seasoning pastes. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_dulce))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cachucha” means **“cap”** or **“hat”** in Spanish, referencing the pepper’s unique flattened shape that looks like a small cap. It is also known as *Ají Dulce* in other parts of the Caribbean. Fun fact: while it has the scent of a super-hot pepper, it is mild enough to be eaten by the handful. Its name captures its **distinctive form and its gentle, aromatic role** in traditional Cuban home cooking. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Aji_Cachucha_Chile_Peppers_11438.php))
shortDescription: Small, mild Cuban pepper with squashed lantern shape and sweet aroma, ideal for sofrito.

name: Aji Cristal
description: **Ají Cristal Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a popular chili from Chile, registering between **30,000 and 50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces 3-4 inch pods that ripen from a translucent pale green to yellow and finally orange-red. The flavor is notably **crisp and citrusy**, with a clean heat that doesn’t linger too long. It is most commonly used fresh in Chilean *pebre* (a traditional salsa) or pickled to preserve its bright color. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/aji-cristal-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Cristal” (Spanish for **“crystal”**) refers to the **translucent, waxy appearance** of the pods during their early stages of ripening. It is the most beloved chili in Chile, found in almost every household and market. Fun fact: locals often prefer eating them at the pale yellow stage when the citrus flavor is most intense. Its name reflects its **shimmering, waxy skin and its vital place** in South American culinary tradition. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/aji-peppers/aji-cristal/))
shortDescription: Popular Chilean chili with translucent waxy skin, citrusy flavor, and moderate heat.

name: Aji Dulce
description: **Ají Dulce Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a small, sweet chili variety native to Venezuela and popular throughout the Caribbean. It has almost no heat (**0–500 SHU**), despite looking nearly identical to a habanero. The flavor is exceptionally **aromatic, sweet, and fruity**, used primarily as a seasoning pepper. It is the defining ingredient in Venezuelan *hallacas* and Caribbean *sofrito*, where it provides complex flavor without the spice. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_dulce))
aditionalInfo: The name “Ají Dulce” translates literally to **“sweet pepper”** in Spanish (“ají” = pepper, “dulce” = sweet), reflecting its mild heat and gentler flavour profile compared to hotter relatives. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aj%C3%AD_dulce)). Fun fact: it is a cornerstone of Caribbean cuisine, especially used in the sofrito base in Puerto Rican and Cuban cooking, where it provides aroma and flavour without overwhelming spice. ([Epicurious](https://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/puerto-rican-style-em-aji-dulce-em-sauce-em-ajilimojili-em-51203210)).
shortDescription: Mild and aromatic chili with sweet, fruity flavor, ideal for Caribbean sofrito and seasoning.

name: Aji Fantasy
description: **Aji Fantasy Pepper** is a mild to medium chili from the *Capsicum baccatum* species, developed in Finland through about five years of selective breeding from a Brazilian variety. It registers roughly **50,000–100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are bell-shaped and ripen to a creamy pale yellow, offering a **sweet, crisp flavor with notes of apple and citrus**. It is a favorite among gardeners for its high productivity and unique color. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/aji-fantasy/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Fantasy” reflects the **dreamy, pale yellow color** and the “fantastic” balance of mild heat and extreme sweetness. It was stabilized and popularized by Finnish chili breeder Jukka Kilpinen. Fun fact: it is considered one of the best “snacking” peppers for those who like a little heat, as its crunchy texture and fruitiness make it delicious even when raw. Its name emphasizes its **unique, designer-pedigree and superior flavor profile**. ([Fatalii Seeds](https://www.fatalii.net/aji_fantasy))
shortDescription: Sweet and crisp Brazilian-Finnish chili with bell-shaped pods and mild to medium heat.

name: Aji Habanero
description: **Ají Habanero Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a uniquely named pepper that produces long, finger-like pods despite its name suggesting it is a member of the habanero family. It has a mild to medium heat of **5,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The flavor is **sweet and floral**, typical of baccatum species, with a very high yield of pods that ripen from green to a glossy orange-yellow. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/aji-habanero/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Ají Habanero” is often considered a **botanical misnomer**, as it belongs to the *baccatum* species rather than the *chinense* (habanero) species. The name likely stems from its spicy nature or its Caribbean-style sweetness. Fun fact: it is one of the easiest peppers to grow, producing dozens of pods even in less-than-ideal conditions. Its name reflects its **hybrid culinary character**, blending the shape of an aji with the spirit of a habanero. ([Specialty Produce](https://specialtyproduce.com/produce/Aji_Habanero_Chile_Peppers_11438.php))
shortDescription: Sweet and floral South American chili with finger-like pods and moderate heat.

name: Aji Omnicolor
description: **Ají Omnicolor Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a stunningly beautiful ornamental-culinary chili from Peru, rating around **30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Its pods grow vertically and transition through a **spectacular range of colors**: creamy white, yellow, purple, orange, and finally red. The flavor is typical of an aji—**fruity, citrusy, and bright**—making it as useful in the kitchen as it is in the garden. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/aji-omnicolor/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Omnicolor” translates to **“all colors”**, referencing the plant’s ability to display peppers of five different colors simultaneously. This variety is a favorite for container gardening due to its compact size and high visual impact. Fun fact: the peppers are actually at their most flavorful when they are creamy white/yellow, but they are most decorative at the orange/red stage. Its name perfectly describes its **kaleidoscopic appearance and versatile nature**. ([Fatalii Seeds](https://www.fatalii.net/aji_omnicolor))
shortDescription: Stunning Peruvian chili with vertical pods displaying a range of colors and moderate heat.

name: Aji Pineapple
description: **Ají Pineapple Pepper** (*Capsicum baccatum*) is a specialty chili from Peru, measuring roughly **20,000–30,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It is famous for its **distinctive pineapple-like aroma and flavor**, which is far more pronounced than other fruity chilies. The pods are long, slender, and ripen to a brilliant yellow, delivering a sharp but manageable heat that is ideal for fresh salsas and tropical hot sauces. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/aji-pineapple/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Ají Pineapple” is a direct reference to the **uncanny fruitiness** of the pod, which sets it apart from many other baccatum varieties ([Pepper Geek](https://peppergeek.com/aji-pineapple/)). A fun fact: the plants are remarkably productive; one gardener reported dozens of pods on a compact plant, making it an excellent choice for hot‐sauce makers or picklers. They’re also suited to cooler climates compared to many peppers of their kind ([PepperPulse](https://www.pepperpulse.com/aji-pineapple/)).
shortDescription: Medium-hot chili with fruity and citrusy flavor, ideal for those seeking a colorful and flavorful pepper.

name: Apache
description: **Apache Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a compact & prolific chili variety, producing bullet‑shaped pods about 1‑2 inches (2.5‑5 cm) long, ripening from green to bright red, and reaching heat levels of approximately 80,000‑100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU), placing it well into “extra‑hot” territory. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/apache-pepper/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) The plant is ideal for container gardening or small spaces, thanks to its bushy, dwarf habit and heavy fruiting.
aditionalInfo: The name “Apache” likely evokes the bold, fiery character of the pepper rather than a geographical origin. A fun fact: despite its compact size, the Apache pepper can deliver heat comparable to much larger varieties, making it a favourite for hot‑sauce makers and patio growers alike. It also earned the (RHS) Award of Garden Merit for its garden‑performance and reliability.
shortDescription: Compact and prolific chili with intense heat, ideal for container gardening and hot sauces.

name: Bahamian Goat
description: **Bahamian Goat Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a fiery chili from the Bahamas, producing pods roughly 2–3 inches long. It is rated between **100,000 and 350,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, similar to a habanero. The flavor is exceptionally **sweet and tropical**, with a peach-to-orange color that sets it apart in the garden. It is a favorite for traditional Bahamian pepper sauces and stews, providing both heat and a floral complexity. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bahamian-goat-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bahamian Goat” is believed to refer to its **“kick” (like a goat)** or the way the pods look when they are ripening on the plant. It is closely related to the Scotch Bonnet but has a slightly different shape and a more peach-like hue. Fun fact: many chili enthusiasts claim it is one of the most flavorful peppers in the world, often surpassing the habanero in blind taste tests. Its name reflects its **island heritage and its formidable heat level**. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/medium-hot-chili-peppers/bahamian-goat-peppers/))
shortDescription: Fiery Bahamian chili with sweet and tropical flavor, ideal for traditional sauces.

name: Beaver Dam
description: **Beaver Dam Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a large, horn-shaped heirloom chili brought to Beaver Dam, Wisconsin, by Hungarian immigrants in 1912. It registers a very mild **500–1,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are huge, crunchy, and ripen from lime-green to a vibrant red. The flavor is a unique blend of sweet and tangy, making it the perfect pepper for stuffing, roasting, or fresh eating in salads. ([Seed Savers Exchange](https://www.seedsavers.org/beaver-dam-pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Beaver Dam” refers to the **town of Beaver Dam, Wisconsin**, where the pepper has been a local specialty for over a century. It is celebrated every year at a dedicated pepper festival in the town. Fun fact: it is specifically adapted to the shorter growing seasons of the northern US, making it a favorite for gardeners in colder climates. Its name honors its **immigrant history and its status as a beloved regional heirloom**. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/beaver-dam-pepper/))
shortDescription: Large heirloom Hungarian-US chili with mild heat and crunchy texture, ideal for stuffing.

name: Bell Pepper
description: **Bell Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is the world’s most popular sweet pepper, containing zero heat (**0 SHU**). It is defined by its blocky shape, thick walls, and crunchy texture. Bell peppers come in a variety of colors including green, red, yellow, and orange, with each color representing a different stage of maturity and flavor profile (red being the sweetest). They are a nutritional powerhouse, exceptionally high in Vitamin C. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bell Pepper” is a direct reference to its **distinctive bell-like shape**. While it is a member of the *Capsicum* genus, it is the only variety that does not produce capsaicin due to a recessive gene. Fun fact: in many parts of the world, they are simply called “capsicums” or “paprikas.” Its name reflects its **iconic form and its universal status** as the definitive sweet pepper of global cuisine. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/bell-pepper/))
shortDescription: World's most popular sweet pepper with zero heat, defined by its blocky shape and crunchy texture.

name: Bird's Eye
description: **Bird’s Eye Chili** (*Capsicum annuum* or *frutescens*), also known as the *Thai chili*, is a small but potent pepper registering between **50,000 and 100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are thin and pointed, ripening from green to a vivid red. It is a staple ingredient in Southeast Asian and African cuisines, valued for its intense, clean heat and its ability to spice up stir-fries, curries, and dipping sauces. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bird%27s_eye_chili))
aditionalInfo: The name “Bird’s Eye” likely comes from its **small size and the way birds spread the seeds**, as birds are immune to the heat of the pepper. In Thailand, they are known as *phrik khi nu*, which translates to “mouse-dropping chili.” Fun fact: despite their small size, they pack more heat per gram than many larger chilies. Its name signifies its **diminutive stature and its widespread presence** across the world’s tropical regions. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/birds-eye-chili/))
shortDescription: Small but potent Ethiopian-Thai chili with clean heat, staple in Asian and African cuisines.

name: Chilaca
description: **Chilaca Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a long, narrow Mexican chili that is almost always used in its dried form, where it is known as the **Pasilla**. It has a mild heat of **1,000–2,500 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. Fresh chilacas are dark green (nearly black) and have a rich, earthy flavor with hints of berry. They are a core component of traditional Mexican cooking, specifically used for making deep, complex sauces like mole. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chilaca))
aditionalInfo: The name “Chilaca” is derived from the Nahuatl word *chilatl*, meaning **“water chili”**, possibly referring to its juicy nature when fresh. When dried, it is called *pasilla* (“little raisin”) due to its wrinkled, dark skin. Fun fact: it is one of the “holy trinity” of Mexican chilies and is considered essential for an authentic mole poblano. Its name reflects its **ancient Aztec roots and its transformative role** in Mexican gastronomy. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/chilaca-pepper/))
shortDescription: Long, narrow Mexican chili with mild heat and rich, earthy flavor, known as Pasilla when dried.

name: Chiltepin
description: **Chiltepin Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum var. glabriusculum*) is a tiny, round wild chili from Mexico and the American Southwest, often called the “mother of all peppers.” It registers a high **50,000–100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pea-sized pods deliver an immediate, intense heat that dissipates quickly. They are traditionally harvested from the wild, dried, and used as a potent seasoning for soups and stews. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chiltepin))
aditionalInfo: The name “Chiltepin” comes from the Nahuatl *chiltecpintli*, a combination of *chilli* (pepper) and *tecpintl* (flea), referring to its **tiny size and “biting” heat**. It is the only pepper native to the United States and is officially the State Native Pepper of Texas. Fun fact: it is notoriously difficult to cultivate from seed, often requiring birds to eat and digest them first to break the seed coat. Its name honors its **ancient wild heritage and its fierce, fleeting burn**. ([Desert Museum](https://www.desertmuseum.org/kids/oz/long-fact-sheets/Chiltepin.php))
shortDescription: Tiny wild Mexican-US chili with intense, quick-dissipating heat, known as the 'mother of all peppers'.

name: Chinese Five Color
description: **Chinese Five Color Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a spectacular ornamental-culinary chili from China, rating between **30,000 and 50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. As the name suggests, the plant produces pods that transition through **five vibrant colors**: purple, cream, yellow, orange, and finally red. The peppers are small and conical, growing upright on a bushy plant with dark foliage. They offer a clean, spicy heat that works well in stir-fries and fresh salsas. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/chinese-five-color-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Chinese Five Color” is a direct reference to the **plant’s multicolored display** and its origins in East Asia. It is prized for its ability to produce hundreds of peppers on a single plant, making it a favorite for both landscape design and hot-sauce making. Fun fact: the plant is so prolific and colorful that it is often mistaken for a flower. Its name captures its **vibrant aesthetic appeal and its high-yielding productivity**. ([Fatalii Seeds](https://www.fatalii.net/chinese_five_color))
shortDescription: Spectacular Chinese chili displaying five vibrant colors with moderate heat.

name: Chocolate Habanero
description: **Chocolate Habanero** (*Capsicum chinense*), also known as the *Congo Black*, is a super-hot variant of the habanero family from the Caribbean. It registers between **300,000 and 450,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, making it significantly hotter than a standard orange habanero. The pods are larger and ripen to a deep chocolate brown, offering a **rich, smoky, and earthy flavor** that is prized for making authentic Jamaican jerk sauces. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chocolate_habanero))
aditionalInfo: The name “Chocolate Habanero” refers to its **unique dark brown color**, not its flavor. However, many enthusiasts do note a deeper, more “earthy” profile compared to other habaneros. In Jamaica, it is often called the “Congo Black.” Fun fact: it takes much longer to mature than other habaneros, but the wait is considered worth it for its intense heat and complex taste. Its name reflects its **striking dark appearance and its formidable spicy reputation**. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/chocolate-habanero-chili-peppers/))
shortDescription: Super-hot Jamaican chili with deep chocolate brown color and rich, smoky flavor.

name: Corbaci
description: **Corbaci Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender and elongated heirloom sweet pepper from Turkey, with fruits reaching up to 30 cm long and ripening from pale green to yellow and then bright red. They have a mild heat—often under 1,000 Scoville Heat Units—and a rich, sweet flavor with thin walls ideal for fresh eating, frying or drying. ([TomatoFifou](https://www.tomatofifou.com/en/produit/corbaci/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Corbaci” derives from the Turkish word *çorba* meaning “soup,” because these peppers were traditionally served alongside soups in Turkish eateries. One fun fact: despite its length and delicate shape—often curving like a scimitar—the Corbaci pepper is extremely prolific, with plants yielding high numbers of fruits and often requiring support. Often grown as an heirloom garden specialty, it’s also valued for pickling and making colorful sweet pepper powders. ([SmartGardener](https://www.smartgardener.com/plants/4143-peppers-corbaci/overview))
shortDescription: Slender and elongated sweet pepper with rich flavor, ideal for fresh eating and frying.

name: Costa Rican Sweet
description: **Costa Rican Sweet Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a pleasantly sweet, elongated variety that produces ruby‑red pods reaching about 6 inches (~15 cm) in length. The flesh is medium‑thick and very flavorful, often described as richer than standard sweet bells. ([Ball Seed](https://www.ballseed.com/PlantInfo/?phid=048300001023098)) It matures in approximately 70 days and performs well in garden beds or containers, making it ideal for salads, roasting or fresh eating. ([Reimer Seeds](https://www.reimerseeds.com/costa-rican-pepper-seeds))
aditionalInfo: The name “Costa Rican Sweet” highlights the variety’s association with Costa Rica, though it’s broadly grown in warm‑climate regions. A fun fact: despite its sweet nature, this pepper is a Marconi‑type with impressive yield and versatility—gardeners report prolific harvests of long fruits that hold color and flavour well under heat stress. ([Burpee](https://www.burpee.com/pepper-sweet-costa-rican-sweet-hybrid-prod000828.html)) Its elongated shape also makes it excellent for stuffing or grilling whole, adding a gourmet touch to home‑grown peppers.
shortDescription: Sweet and flavorful pepper with medium-thick flesh, ideal for salads and roasting.

name: Datil
description: **Datil Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a small but explosive chili, typically measuring 1–4 inches (~2.5–10 cm) in length and natively ripening to a bright yellow or golden‑orange color with a date‑like shape. Its heat ranges from **100,000 to 300,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, similar to a habanero, but it is prized for its exceptionally **sweet, fruity, and slightly nutty flavor**. It is primarily grown in the St. Augustine area of Florida, where it is a cultural icon used in local hot sauces and relishes. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Datil_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Datil” is the **Spanish word for “date”**, referring to the fruit’s elongated, date-like appearance. Its origin is a subject of local legend in Florida—some say it was brought by Minorcan settlers in the 18th century, while others suggest it came from Cuba. Fun fact: St. Augustine holds an annual Datil Pepper Festival every October to celebrate its unique heritage. Its name reflects both its **distinctive shape and its deep connection** to the history of the nation’s oldest city. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/datil-pepper/))
shortDescription: Small but explosive US chili with sweet, fruity, and nutty flavor, iconic to St. Augustine, Florida.

name: Devil's Tongue
description: **Devil's Tongue Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili variety discovered in Pennsylvania, measuring roughly **125,000–325,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are long, wrinkled, and tapered, resembling a tongue. It offers a **bright, citrusy, and sweet flavor** that is much sharper and fruitier than a standard habanero. It typically ripens to a vibrant yellow, though red and chocolate variants exist. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/devils-tongue-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Devil’s Tongue” is a reference to the **pod’s long, tapered shape** and its “devilish” level of heat. It was first discovered growing in a field of habaneros by a Pennsylvania farmer in the 1990s. Fun fact: it is considered one of the most flavorful yellow super-hots, often used to make citrus-based hot sauces and salsas. Its name perfectly captures the **pepper’s intimidating appearance and its fiery, aggressive burn**. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/superhot-chili-peppers/devils-tongue-chili-peppers/))
shortDescription: Ultra-hot US chili with long, tapered pods and bright, citrusy flavor.

name: Elephant Trunk
description: **Elephant Trunk Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a unique chili from India, producing exceptionally long pods that can reach up to 12 inches. It carries a mild heat of **500–2,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, similar to a mild Anaheim. The pods are thin-walled, twisted, and ripen from green to a deep red. The flavor is sweet and clean, making it an excellent pepper for frying, roasting, or drying for mild chili powder. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/elephant-trunk-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Elephant Trunk” is a direct reference to its **long, wrinkled, and often curved shape** which looks like the trunk of an elephant. It is a highly productive plant, often needing support due to the weight of its massive fruit. Fun fact: in India, it is often used as a “base” pepper for curries where flavor is needed without intense spice. Its name captures the **pepper’s impressive size and its distinct, playful appearance**. ([Seed Savers Exchange](https://www.seedsavers.org/elephant-trunk-pepper))
shortDescription: Unique Indian chili with exceptionally long pods and mild heat, ideal for frying and roasting.

name: Fatalii
description: **Fatalii Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra-hot chili from central and southern Africa, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **125,000 to 325,000 SHU**. It produces thin-walled, yellow pods with a **bright, citrusy, and fruity flavor**. Often used in **hot sauces and seafood dishes**, it is prized for its intense, immediate heat ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fatalii), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/fatalii-chili-pepper/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Fatalii”** reflects its **African origins** and its reputation for a “fatal” level of heat ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fatalii)). Unlike many other habanero-type peppers, the Fatalii’s heat is felt instantly at the front of the mouth. Known for its **distinctive citrus aroma**, it is often considered the best yellow pepper for making tropical sauces. Its name reflects both its **vibrant flavor and its aggressive heat profile** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/fatalii-chili-pepper/)).
shortDescription: Ultra-hot African chili with thin-walled yellow pods and bright, citrusy flavor.

name: Habanero
description: **Habanero Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is one of the most popular hot chili peppers in the world, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **100,000 to 350,000 SHU**. It produces small, lantern-shaped pods with a **fruity, floral, and citrusy flavor**. A staple in Mexican and Caribbean cuisines, it is used in **salsas, hot sauces, and marinades** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Habanero), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/habanero-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Habanero”** means **“from Havana”**, referencing the Cuban city where it was once traded extensively ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Habanero)). It is natively grown in the Yucatán Peninsula of Mexico. Known for its **versatility and intense heat**, it is the gold standard for many hot sauce makers. The name reflects both its **historic trade roots and its global status as a premium hot chili** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/habanero-peppers/)).
shortDescription: One of the most popular hot chilies in the world with fruity, floral flavor and intense heat.

name: Jalapeño
description: **Jalapeño Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is the world's most famous chili pepper, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **2,500 to 8,000 SHU**. It produces medium-sized pods with a **crisp, grassy, and slightly sweet flavor**. A staple in Mexican and American cuisines, it is used in **nachos, salsas, poppers, and pickling** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jalape%C3%B1o), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/jalapeno-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Jalapeño”** means **“from Xalapa”**, the capital city of Veracruz, Mexico, where it was traditionally cultivated ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jalape%C3%B1o)). It is unique because it is often consumed while green, though it turns red and becomes sweeter as it matures. Known for its **approachable heat and culinary flexibility**, it is the quintessential chili for many. Its name reflects both its **geographic origin and its iconic status in world cuisine** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/jalapeno-peppers/)).
shortDescription: World's most famous chili with crisp, grassy flavor and approachable heat.

name: Joe's Long Cayenne
description: **Joe's Long Cayenne Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an heirloom variety famous for its incredible length, often reaching up to 12 inches. It measures between **30,000 and 50,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**, similar to a standard cayenne. The pods are thin-walled, ripening from green to a vivid red, and are prized for their **sharp, clean heat and easy-to-dry nature**. It is a favorite for making homemade chili flakes and powders. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/joes-long-cayenne-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Joe’s Long” honors **Joe Sestito**, an Italian immigrant who brought the seeds to the United States and shared them with the chili community. It is a highly productive plant, often producing dozens of massive pods. Fun fact: despite their length, the pods are quite slender and can be used in any recipe calling for a traditional cayenne. Its name reflects its **immigrant history and its impressive, record-breaking size**. ([Seed Savers Exchange](https://www.seedsavers.org/joes-long-cayenne-pepper))
shortDescription: Heirloom Italian-US chili with exceptionally long pods and sharp, clean heat.

name: Madame Jeanette
description: **Madame Jeanette Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an ultra‑hot chili from Suriname, measuring roughly **125,000–325,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces elongated, wrinkled pods that ripen to a brilliant yellow. The flavor is remarkably **fruity and aromatic**, with notes of mango and pineapple that accompany its intense heat. It is an essential ingredient in Surinamese and Antillean cuisines, used for hot sauces, pickles, and spicy stews. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Madame_Jeanette))
aditionalInfo: The name “Madame Jeanette” is rumored to be named after a **famous prostitute from Paramaribo**, Suriname, whose “fire” matched the intensity of the pepper. It is often confused with the yellow habanero, but it has a more elongated shape and a more complex, floral aroma. Fun fact: it is considered one of the most flavorful chilies in the world and is a source of national pride in Suriname. Its name reflects its **colorful cultural history and its legendary flavor-to-heat ratio**. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/madame-jeanette-pepper/))
shortDescription: Ultra-hot Surinamese chili with elongated yellow pods and fruity, aromatic flavor.

name: Malagueta
description: **Malagueta Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a small, tapered chili that is a defining ingredient in Brazilian and Portuguese cuisines. It registers a high **50,000–100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are roughly 2 inches long and ripen from green to a bright red. It offers a **sharp, clean heat with subtle fruity undertones**, commonly used in marinades (pimenta), seafood stews (moqueca), and spicy oils. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Malagueta_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Malagueta” is often confused with the unrelated West African spice “melegueta pepper” (grains of paradise). It was spread globally by Portuguese explorers and became a staple in West Africa and Southeast Asia. Fun fact: in Brazil, the smallest, hottest pods are known as *malaguetinha*. Its name signifies its **historic role as a global traveler and its essential place** in the spice cultures of the Lusosphere. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/malagueta-pepper/))
shortDescription: Small Brazilian-Portuguese chili with sharp heat and subtle fruity flavor, ideal for marinades.

name: Piri-Piri
description: **Piri‑Piri Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a small yet intensely hot chili, typically around 2–3 cm long, ripening from green to vivid red, and commonly rated between 50,000 and 175,000 Scoville Heat Units. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piri_piri)) It offers a sharp, straight‑to‑the‑point heat with a clean pepper flavour and thin flesh, making it ideal for drying, ground spice blends or bold hot sauces.
aditionalInfo: The name “piri‑piri” comes from the Swahili word *pili pili* meaning “pepper,” which Portuguese explorers adopted as they spread the pepper into Africa and on to Europe. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piri_piri)) Fun fact: this pepper is the key ingredient in the classic Portuguese dish “frango piri‑piri,” and its spread throughout the former Portuguese colonies helped turn it into a global hot‑sauce staple.
shortDescription: Small yet intensely hot chili with clean pepper flavor, ideal for drying and hot sauces.

name: Jindungo
description: **Jindungo Pepper** (*Capsicum frutescens*) is a compact, potent chili variety native to Angola and widely used in Portuguese‑speaking regions. Its tiny pods, roughly 1–3 cm in length, ripen to vivid red and pack intense heat—often exceeding 50,000 Scoville Heat Units. ([DHotSeeds](https://dhotseeds.com/en/more-hot-peppers/48-jindungo-10-seeds.html)) Known for its clean, fiery flavour and generous seed count, it’s ideal for drying and grinding into spicy condiments.
aditionalInfo: The name “Jindungo” is a regional term in Angola used for what’s also called “piri‑piri” or “malagueta” in other lusophone countries. ([Wikipedia Pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pimenta-malagueta)) One fun fact: the pepper is a culinary staple in Angolan cuisine, traditionally used fresh in stews like muamba and served as a fiery sauce‑base. It is reputed to have medicinal qualities, including aiding digestion and circulation, and some local sources suggest that regular consumption may even provide mild analgesic benefits.
shortDescription: Compact and potent chili with clean, fiery flavor, ideal for drying and spicy condiments.

name: Maule's Red Hot
description: **Maule’s Red Hot Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an American heirloom chili introduced by the William Maule Seed Company of Philadelphia in the early 1900s. ([Seedways](https://www.seedways.org/product-page/maule-s-red-hot-pepper)) The plant produces long, slender pods—typically 10–12 inches (25–30 cm) in length—that mature from green to bright red. With a heat range around **15,000–50,000 SHU**, it provides a sharp, clean burn, and its thin walls make it excellent for drying, frying, or creating spicy sauces.
aditionalInfo: The name “Maule’s Red Hot” commemorates its namesake seed company and the pepper’s vibrant crimson hue and punchy heat level. Historical catalogues from the turn of the century praised it for its early maturation and massive yield, making it a favorite for market gardeners. Fun fact: it’s often noted for its versatility in the kitchen, performing just as well fresh as it does when dried into flakes. Its name reflects its **heritage as a staple of early-20th-century American gardening culture**.
shortDescription: American heirloom chili with long, slender pods and sharp, clean heat.

name: Mulato
description: **Mulato Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild Mexican chili that is a close relative of the Poblano. It is almost always used in its dried form, where it registers between **1,000 and 2,500 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The dried pods are large, wrinkled, and nearly black, offering a **deep, smoky flavor with hints of chocolate and licorice**. It is one of the “holy trinity” of Mexican dried chilies, essential for authentic moles and adobos. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mulato_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Mulato” is a Spanish term referring to its **dark brown, nearly black color** when dried. It is genetically similar to the Poblano, but it contains different levels of tannins, which give it its distinctive chocolate-like undertones. Fun fact: while the Ancho (dried Poblano) is sweet and fruity, the Mulato is smoky and earthy, providing the necessary depth to Mexican sauces. Its name reflects its **dark, rich appearance and its essential role** in the complex flavor chemistry of Mexican cuisine. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/mulato-pepper/))
shortDescription: Mild Mexican dried chili with deep, smoky flavor and hints of chocolate, essential for moles.

name: NuMex Heritage Big Jim
description: **NuMex Heritage Big Jim Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a refined version of the original Big Jim, bred by New Mexico State University to restore the variety’s traditional flavor and heat level (**500–3,000 SHU**). It produces massive, 10–12 inch pods that are the gold standard for roasting and making green chili stew. The flavor is a perfect balance of **sweetness, earthiness, and mild spice**, with thick walls that hold up perfectly during grilling. ([NMSU Chile Pepper Institute](https://cpi.nmsu.edu/chile-info/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Heritage” signifies the **preservation of the variety’s original traits**, which had become inconsistent over decades of mass production. It pays tribute to the “Big Jim” Lytle family and their contribution to New Mexico’s chili industry. Fun fact: this pepper is often the centerpiece of the annual Chile Roast in New Mexico, where its massive size makes it a crowd favorite. Its name reflects its **scientific pedigree and its role as a guardian of Southwestern culinary tradition**. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/numex-heritage-big-jim-pepper/))
shortDescription: Refined version of the Big Jim US chili with traditional flavor and mild heat, ideal for roasting.

name: Orange Thai
description: **Orange Thai Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a vibrant, high-heat chili from Thailand, registering between **50,000 and 100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces 2–3 inch pods that ripen from green to a brilliant, glowing orange. The flavor is exceptionally **fruity and bright**, providing a slightly different aromatic profile than the traditional red Thai bird’s eye chili. It is a favorite for adding heat and color to Thai salads, stir-fries, and spicy oils. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/orange-thai-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Orange Thai” is a direct reference to its **distinctive neon-orange color** and its geographic origin. It is a highly ornamental plant, producing hundreds of upright-growing pods. Fun fact: despite its beauty, it is every bit as hot as its red counterparts, often surprising those who expect a milder taste from orange peppers. Its name signifies its **vibrant aesthetic and its essential role** in the spicy, colorful palette of Thai street food. ([Fatalii Seeds](https://www.fatalii.net/orange_thai))
shortDescription: Vibrant high-heat Thai chili with brilliant orange pods and fruity flavor.

name: Padron
description: **Padron Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a famous chili from the Galicia region of Spain, registering between **500 and 2,500 SHU**. It produces small, conical, green pods with a **sweet and nutty flavor**. Known for the “Russian Roulette” nature of its heat, as one in every ten peppers is significantly hotter than the others. A staple in Spanish tapas, it is traditionally **blistered in olive oil and sprinkled with sea salt** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Padr%C3%B3n_pepper), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/mild-chili-peppers/padron-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Padron”** refers to the **town of Padrón in Spain**, where these peppers were first cultivated by Franciscan monks in the 16th century ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Padr%C3%B3n_pepper)). They are historically significant and have a dedicated festival every August. Known for their **culinary unpredictability**, they are a source of both enjoyment and surprise in Spanish cuisine. The name reflects both its **geographic origin and its status as a legendary tapas staple** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/mild-chili-peppers/padron-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Famous Spanish chili with 'Russian Roulette' heat and nutty flavor, staple in tapas.

name: Poblano
description: **Poblano Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a large, heart-shaped chili pepper from Mexico, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **1,000 to 2,000 SHU**. It produces thick-walled, dark green pods with a **rich, earthy, and smoky flavor**. When dried, it is known as an **Ancho chili**. A staple in Mexican cuisine, it is famously used in **Chiles Rellenos and mole sauces** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poblano), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/mild-chili-peppers/poblano-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Poblano”** means **“from Puebla”**, the Mexican state where it originated ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poblano)). It is one of the most popular peppers in Mexico due to its mildness and deep flavor. Known for its **versatility and size**, it is perfect for stuffing. Its name reflects both its **geographic roots and its essential role in traditional Mexican culinary arts** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/mild-chili-peppers/poblano-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Large Mexican chili with rich, earthy flavor and mild heat, known as Ancho when dried.

name: Red Savina Habanero
description: **Red Savina Habanero** (*Capsicum chinense*) is an exceptionally hot chili variety that held the world record for heat for over a decade. It registers between **350,000 and 577,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. The pods are larger, redder, and more pungent than a standard habanero, offering a **potent fruity sweetness followed by a massive, lingering burn**. It was developed in California through selective breeding for size and intensity. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red_Savina_pepper))
aditionalInfo: The name “Red Savina” is a **portmanteau of the creator’s mother’s name (Savina)** and the pepper’s deep red color. Developed by Frank Garcia of GNS Spices, it was protected by a Plant Variety Protection act for many years. Fun fact: it was the first pepper to truly challenge the idea that a “standard” chili could be record-breaking. Its name signifies its **legendary status and its place as the predecessor** to the modern super-hot era. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/red-savina-habanero/))
shortDescription: Former world record holder US chili with deep red pods and intense fruity sweetness.

name: Rocoto
description: **Rocoto Pepper** (*Capsicum pubescens*) is a unique chili originating from the Andes Mountains, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **30,000 to 100,000 SHU**. It produces round, thick-walled pods with **black seeds and hairy leaves**, characteristic of its species. Known for its **juicy texture and clean, fruity flavor**, it is used in **salsas, hot sauces, and traditional Andean dishes** like *Rocoto Relleno* ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rocoto_pepper), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/rocoto-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Rocoto”** is the common name used in Peru and Bolivia for this pepper ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rocoto_pepper)). It is one of the oldest domesticated chilies, dating back thousands of years. Unlike most peppers, it can **tolerate cooler temperatures and light frost**. Known for its **impressive heat and black seeds**, it is a cultural icon in the Andes. Its name reflects its **ancient South American heritage and its unique botanical identity** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/rocoto-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Unique Andean chili with black seeds, hairy leaves, and juicy, fruity flavor.

name: Scotch Bonnet
description: **Scotch Bonnet Pepper** (*Capsicum chinense*) is a hot chili pepper essential to Caribbean cuisine, with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **100,000 to 350,000 SHU**. It produces squashed, bonnet-shaped pods with a **sweet, fruity, and tropical flavor**. A staple in Jamaica, it is the key ingredient in **jerk seasoning, hot sauces, and Caribbean stews** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scotch_bonnet), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/scotch-bonnet-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Scotch Bonnet”** reflects its resemblance to a **traditional Scottish tam o'shanter hat** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scotch_bonnet)). It is closely related to the habanero but is prized for its **superior sweetness and floral aroma**. Known for its **definitive role in Caribbean jerk cooking**, it is synonymous with island flavor. The name reflects both its **distinctive appearance and its legendary culinary status in the West Indies** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/scotch-bonnet-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Essential Jamaican chili with squashed bonnet shape and sweet, tropical flavor.

name: Serrano
description: **Serrano Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, vibrant chili originating from the mountainous regions of Puebla and Hidalgo in Mexico. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serrano_pepper)) Typically harvested while green but also available in red, brown, or yellow, it registers between **10,000 and 25,000 Scoville Heat Units**—hotter than a jalapeño but milder than many super‑hots. ([PepperGeek](https://peppergeek.com/serrano-peppers/)) The flavour is crisp and bright, with a sharp peppery bite that works beautifully diced in salsas, pickles or fresh tacos.
aditionalInfo: The name “serrano” derives from the Spanish word for “highland” or “mountain” (serra/sierra), referring to the elevated terrain where it was traditionally grown. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serrano_pepper)) A fun fact: each plant can produce dozens of pods—some yield 50 or more—and while most fruits deliver consistent heat, experienced eaters note that occasional outliers can surprise with a sharper punch. The pepper is hugely popular in Mexican cuisine and is now also grown for export worldwide.
shortDescription: Slender Mexican chili with crisp, bright flavor and moderate heat, hotter than a jalapeño.

name: Shishito
description: **Shishito Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a mild Japanese chili with Scoville Heat Units ranging from **50 to 200 SHU**. It produces small, wrinkled, green pods with a **sweet and grassy flavor**. Known for the occasional “spicy surprise” (one in ten is hot). A staple in Japanese cuisine, it is traditionally **blistered in a pan and served with soy sauce** ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shishito), [Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/shishito-chili-peppers/)).
aditionalInfo: The name **“Shishito”** comes from the Japanese words for **“lion head”** (*shishi* + *togarashi*), referencing the tip of the pepper which resembles a lion’s snout ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shishito)). Known for its **thin skin and delicate flavor**, it is a popular appetizer in izakayas. The name reflects both its **distinctive appearance and its status as a quintessential Japanese snack** ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/sweet-mild-chili-peppers/shishito-chili-peppers/)).
shortDescription: Mild Japanese chili with sweet, grassy flavor, traditionally blistered and served as an appetizer.

name: Thai Dragon
description: **Thai Dragon Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is an exceptionally hot chili variety from Thailand, registering between **50,000 and 100,000 Scoville Heat Units (SHU)**. It produces long, slender, and curved pods that grow in clusters and ripen to a fiery red. The flavor is clean and sharp, providing an immediate, intense heat that is fundamental to authentic Thai curries, stir-fries, and spicy salads. ([PepperScale](https://pepperscale.com/thai-dragon-pepper/))
aditionalInfo: The name “Thai Dragon” reflects its **geographic origin and its “mythical” heat level**, suggesting the breath of a dragon. It is a highly productive plant, often used by home gardeners in pots due to its ornamental beauty and heavy yield. Fun fact: despite being a member of the bird’s eye family, the Dragon variant is often larger and slightly more pungent. Its name signifies its **status as a powerhouse of Thai spice** and its aggressive, stinging heat profile. ([Chili Pepper Madness](https://www.chilipeppermadness.com/chili-pepper-types/hot-chili-peppers/thai-dragon-peppers/))
shortDescription: Sleek chili with fierce heat and crisp flavor, ideal for Asian-style dishes.

name: Cayenne
description: **Cayenne Pepper** (*Capsicum annuum*) is a slender, tapering chili variety typically measuring 10–25 cm in length and ripening to a vibrant red. It registers around 30,000–50,000 Scoville Heat Units, making it a hot but approachable chili for many. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cayenne_pepper)) Its thin walls and high capsaicin content make it ideal for drying and grinding into the widely used cayenne powder, a staple spice for cuisines worldwide.
aditionalInfo: The name “cayenne” is believed to derive from the Old Tupi word *kyynha*, meaning “capsicum,” and also shares its name with the city of Cayenne in French Guiana, which may have contributed to the spice’s term. ([Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cayenne_pepper)) Fun fact: while most cayenne peppers are red, yellow and purple variants exist too, and in the 19th century yellow types were quite common. Dried and crushed cayennes are the basis of many “red pepper” condiments, including hot sauces like the classic American favourite.
shortDescription: Slender red chili with sharp heat, commonly dried and ground into spice powder.
